title: Hands-on Exercise 8: Visualising and Analysing Time-Oriented Data
text:
Create the followings data visualisation by using R packages:

plotting a calender heatmap by using ggplot2 functions,
plotting a cycle plot by using ggplot2 function,
plotting a slopegraph
plotting a horizon chart




Code chunk below check, install and launch the following R packages: scales, viridis, lubridate, ggthemes, gridExtra, readxl, knitr, data.table and tidyverse.

pacman::p_load(scales, viridis, lubridate, ggthemes,
               gridExtra, readxl, knitr, data.table,
               CGPfunctions, ggHoriPlot, tidyverse)

Troubleshooting: run following code if unable to load CGPfunctions (optional)

# 1. Install the remotes package
install.packages("remotes")

# 2. Force install 'ggmosaic' from GitHub since CRAN won't find it
remotes::install_github("haleyjeppson/ggmosaic")

# 3. Install 'CGPfunctions' from GitHub
remotes::install_github("ibecav/CGPfunctions")




This section plots a calender heatmap programmatically by using ggplot2 package.

Knowledge to learn:

plot a calender heatmap by using ggplot2 functions and extension,
to write function using R programming,
to derive specific date and time related field by using base R and lubridate packages



For the purpose of this hands-on exercise, eventlog.csv file will be used. This data file consists of 199,999 rows of time-series cyber attack records by country.



First, you will use the code chunk below to import eventlog.csv file into R environment and called the data frame as attacks.

to perform data preparation task by using tidyr and dplyr packages.


attacks <- read_csv("data/eventlog.csv")




It is always a good practice to examine the imported data frame before further analysis is performed.
Following code chunk use kable() to review the structure of the imported data frame.

kable(head(attacks))




timestamp
source_country
tz




2015-03-12 15:59:16
CN
Asia/Shanghai


2015-03-12 16:00:48
FR
Europe/Paris


2015-03-12 16:02:26
CN
Asia/Shanghai


2015-03-12 16:02:38
US
America/Chicago


2015-03-12 16:03:22
CN
Asia/Shanghai


2015-03-12 16:03:45
CN
Asia/Shanghai





There are three columns, namely timestamp, source_country and tz.

timestamp field stores date-time values in POSIXct format.
source_country field stores the source of the attack. It is in ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country code.
tz field stores time zone of the source IP address.




Step 1: Deriving weekday and hour of day fields
Before we can plot the calender heatmap, two new fields namely wkday and hour need to be derived. In this step, we will write a function to perform the task.

make_hr_wkday <- function(ts, sc, tz) {
  real_times <- ymd_hms(ts, 
                        tz = tz[1], 
                        quiet = TRUE)
  dt <- data.table(
    source_country = sc, 
    wkday = weekdays(as.Date(real_times)), 
    hour = hour(real_times)
  )
  return(dt)
}







Note




ymd_hms() and hour() are from lubridate package, and
weekdays() is a base R function.



Step 2: Deriving the attacks tibble data frame

wkday_levels <- c('Saturday', 'Friday', 
                  'Thursday', 'Wednesday', 
                  'Tuesday', 'Monday', 
                  'Sunday')

attacks <- attacks %>%
  group_by(tz) %>%
  do(make_hr_wkday(.$timestamp, 
                   .$source_country, 
                   .$tz)) %>% 
  ungroup() %>% 
  mutate(wkday = factor(
    wkday, levels = wkday_levels),
    hour  = factor(
      hour, levels = 0:23))







Note



Beside extracting the necessary data into attacks data frame, mutate() of dplyr package is used to convert wkday and hour fields into factor so they’ll be ordered when plotting


Table below shows the tidy tibble table after processing.

kable(head(attacks))




tz
source_country
wkday
hour




Africa/Cairo
BG
Saturday
20


Africa/Cairo
TW
Sunday
6


Africa/Cairo
TW
Sunday
8


Africa/Cairo
CN
Sunday
11


Africa/Cairo
US
Sunday
15


Africa/Cairo
CA
Monday
11









grouped <- attacks %>% 
  count(wkday, hour) %>% 
  ungroup() %>%
  na.omit()

ggplot(grouped, 
       aes(hour, 
           wkday, 
           fill = n)) + 
  geom_tile(color = "white", 
            size = 0.1) + 
  theme_tufte(base_family = "Helvetica") + 
  coord_equal() +
  scale_fill_gradient(name = "# of attacks",
                      low = "sky blue", 
                      high = "dark blue") +
  labs(x = NULL, 
       y = NULL, 
       title = "Attacks by weekday and time of day") +
  theme(axis.ticks = element_blank(),
        plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
        legend.title = element_text(size = 8),
        legend.text = element_text(size = 6) )














TipThings to learn from the code chunk




a tibble data table called grouped is derived by aggregating the attack by wkday and hour fields.
a new field called n is derived by using group_by() and count() functions.
na.omit() is used to exclude missing value.
geom_tile() is used to plot tiles (grids) at each x and y position. color and size arguments are used to specify the border color and line size of the tiles.
theme_tufte() of ggthemes package is used to remove unnecessary chart junk. To learn which visual components of default ggplot2 have been excluded, you are encouraged to comment out this line to examine the default plot.
coord_equal() is used to ensure the plot will have an aspect ratio of 1:1.
scale_fill_gradient() function is used to creates a two colour gradient (low-high).



Then we can simply group the count by hour and wkday and plot it, since we know that we have values for every combination there’s no need to further preprocess the data.



Step 1: Deriving attack by country object
In order to identify the top 4 countries with the highest number of attacks, you are required to do the followings:

count the number of attacks by country,
calculate the percent of attackes by country, and
save the results in a tibble data frame.


attacks_by_country <- count(
  attacks, source_country) %>%
  mutate(percent = percent(n/sum(n))) %>%
  arrange(desc(n))

Step 2: Preparing the tidy data frame
Extract the attack records of the top 4 countries from attacks data frame and save the data in a new tibble data frame (i.e. top4_attacks).

top4 <- attacks_by_country$source_country[1:4]
top4_attacks <- attacks %>%
  filter(source_country %in% top4) %>%
  count(source_country, wkday, hour) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(source_country = factor(
    source_country, levels = top4)) %>%
  na.omit()

Step 3: Plotting the Multiple Calender Heatmap by using ggplot2 package.

ggplot(top4_attacks, 
       aes(hour, 
           wkday, 
           fill = n)) + 
  geom_tile(color = "white", 
            size = 0.1) + 
  theme_tufte(base_family = "Helvetica") + 
  coord_equal() +
  scale_fill_gradient(name = "# of attacks",
                      low = "sky blue", 
                      high = "dark blue") +
  facet_wrap(~source_country, ncol = 2) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, 
       title = "Attacks on top 4 countries by weekday and time of day") +
  theme(axis.ticks = element_blank(),
        axis.text.x = element_text(size = 7),
        plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
        legend.title = element_text(size = 8),
        legend.text = element_text(size = 6) )












This section demonstrates how to plot a cycle plot showing the time-series patterns and trend of visitor arrivals from Vietnam programmatically by using ggplot2 functions.



For the purpose of this hands-on exercise, arrivals_by_air.xlsx will be used.
The code chunk below imports arrivals_by_air.xlsx by using read_excel() of readxl package and save it as a tibble data frame called air.

air <- read_excel("data/arrivals_by_air.xlsx")




Next, two new fields called month and year are derived from Month-Year field.

air$month <- factor(month(air$`Month-Year`), 
                    levels=1:12, 
                    labels=month.abb, 
                    ordered=TRUE) 
air$year <- year(ymd(air$`Month-Year`))




Next, the code chunk below is use to extract data for the target country (i.e. Vietnam)

Vietnam <- air %>% 
  select(`Vietnam`, 
         month, 
         year) %>%
  filter(year >= 2010)




The code chunk below uses group_by() and summarise() of dplyr to compute year average arrivals by month.

hline.data <- Vietnam %>% 
  group_by(month) %>%
  summarise(avgvalue = mean(`Vietnam`))




The code chunk below is used to plot the cycle plot as shown at the beginning of section 16.4.

ggplot() + 
  geom_line(data=Vietnam,
            aes(x=year, 
                y=`Vietnam`, 
                group=month), 
            colour="black") +
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept=avgvalue), 
             data=hline.data, 
             linetype=6, 
             colour="red", 
             size=0.5) + 
  facet_grid(~month) +
  labs(title = "Visitor arrivals from Vietnam by air, Jan 2010-Dec 2019") +
  xlab("") +
  ylab("No. of Visitors") +
  theme_tufte(base_family = "Helvetica") + 
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)) # Applies label tilt












This section demonstrates how to plot a slopegraph by using R.
Refer to Using newggslopegraph to learn more about the function. Lastly, read more about newggslopegraph() and its arguments by referring to this link.


Import the rice data set into R environment by using the code chunk below.

rice <- read_csv("data/rice.csv")




Next, code chunk below will be used to plot a basic slopegraph as shown below.

rice %>% 
  mutate(Year = factor(Year)) %>%
  filter(Year %in% c(1961, 1980)) %>%
  newggslopegraph(Year, Yield, Country,
                  Title = "Rice Yield of Top 11 Asian Counties",
                  SubTitle = "1961-1980",
                  Caption = "Prepared using RStudio")














Tip



For effective data visualisation design, factor() is used convert the value type of Year field from numeric to factor.

This section demonstrates how to plot a cycle plot showing the time-series patterns and trend of visitor arrivals from Vietnam programmatically by using ggplot2 functions.



For the purpose of this hands-on exercise, arrivals_by_air.xlsx will be used.
The code chunk below imports arrivals_by_air.xlsx by using read_excel() of readxl package and save it as a tibble data frame called air.

air <- read_excel("data/arrivals_by_air.xlsx")




Next, two new fields called month and year are derived from Month-Year field.

air$month <- factor(month(air$`Month-Year`), 
                    levels=1:12, 
                    labels=month.abb, 
                    ordered=TRUE) 
air$year <- year(ymd(air$`Month-Year`))




Next, the code chunk below is use to extract data for the target country (i.e. Vietnam)

Vietnam <- air %>% 
  select(`Vietnam`, 
         month, 
         year) %>%
  filter(year >= 2010)




The code chunk below uses group_by() and summarise() of dplyr to compute year average arrivals by month.

hline.data <- Vietnam %>% 
  group_by(month) %>%
  summarise(avgvalue = mean(`Vietnam`))




The code chunk below is used to plot the cycle plot as shown at the beginning of section 16.4.

ggplot() + 
  geom_line(data=Vietnam,
            aes(x=year, 
                y=`Vietnam`, 
                group=month), 
            colour="black") +
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept=avgvalue), 
             data=hline.data, 
             linetype=6, 
             colour="red", 
             size=0.5) + 
  facet_grid(~month) +

title: Hands-on Exercise 9b: Visualising Geospatial Point Data
text:
Proportional symbol maps (also known as graduate symbol maps) are a class of maps that use the visual variable of size to represent differences in the magnitude of a discrete, abruptly changing phenomenon, e.g. counts of people. Like choropleth maps, you can create classed or unclassed versions of these maps. The classed ones are known as range-graded or graduated symbols, and the unclassed are called proportional symbols, where the area of the symbols are proportional to the values of the attribute being mapped. This demonstration shows how to create a proportional symbol map showing the number of wins by Singapore Pools’ outlets using an R package called tmap.


This hands-on exercise develops the following skills with appropriate R packages:

To import an aspatial data file into R.
To convert it into simple point feature data frame and at the same time, to assign an appropriate projection reference to the newly create simple point feature data frame.
To plot interactive proportional symbol maps.





Before we get started, we need to ensure that tmap package of R and other related R packages have been installed and loaded into R.

pacman::p_load(sf, tmap, tidyverse)






The data set use for this demonstration is called SGPools_svy21. The data is in csv file format.
Figure below shows the first 15 records of SGPools_svy21.csv. It consists of seven columns. The XCOORD and YCOORD columns are the x-coordinates and y-coordinates of SingPools outlets and branches. They are in Singapore SVY21 Projected Coordinates System.




The code chunk below uses read_csv() function of readr package to import SGPools_svy21.csv into R as a tibble data frame called sgpools.

sgpools <- read_csv("data/aspatial/SGPools_svy21.csv")

After importing the data file into R, it is important to examine if the data file has been imported correctly.
The code chunk below shows list() is used to do the job.

list(sgpools) 

[[1]]
# A tibble: 306 × 7
   NAME           ADDRESS POSTCODE XCOORD YCOORD `OUTLET TYPE` `Gp1Gp2 Winnings`
   <chr>          <chr>      <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl> <chr>                     <dbl>
 1 Livewire (Mar… 2 Bayf…    18972 30842. 29599. Branch                        5
 2 Livewire (Res… 26 Sen…    98138 26704. 26526. Branch                       11
 3 SportsBuzz (K… Lotus …   738078 20118. 44888. Branch                        0
 4 SportsBuzz (P… 1 Sele…   188306 29777. 31382. Branch                       44
 5 Prime Serango… Blk 54…   552542 32239. 39519. Branch                        0
 6 Singapore Poo… 1A Woo…   731001 21012. 46987. Branch                        3
 7 Singapore Poo… Blk 64…   370064 33990. 34356. Branch                       17
 8 Singapore Poo… Blk 88…   370088 33847. 33976. Branch                       16
 9 Singapore Poo… Blk 30…   540308 33910. 41275. Branch                       21
10 Singapore Poo… Blk 20…   560202 29246. 38943. Branch                       25
# ℹ 296 more rows


Notice that the sgpools data in tibble data frame and not the common R data frame.



The code chunk below converts sgpools data frame into a simple feature data frame by using st_as_sf() of sf packages

sgpools_sf <- st_as_sf(sgpools, 
                       coords = c("XCOORD", "YCOORD"),
                       crs= 3414)

Things to learn from the arguments above:

The coords argument requires you to provide the column name of the x-coordinates first then followed by the column name of the y-coordinates.
The crs argument required you to provide the coordinates system in epsg format. EPSG: 3414 is Singapore SVY21 Projected Coordinate System. You can search for other country’s epsg code by refering to epsg.io.

Figure below shows the data table of sgpools_sf. Notice that a new column called geometry has been added into the data frame.

You can display the basic information of the newly created sgpools_sf by using the code chunk below.

list(sgpools_sf)

[[1]]
Simple feature collection with 306 features and 5 fields
Geometry type: POINT
Dimension:     XY
Bounding box:  xmin: 7844.194 ymin: 26525.7 xmax: 45176.57 ymax: 47987.13
Projected CRS: SVY21 / Singapore TM
# A tibble: 306 × 6
   NAME                         ADDRESS POSTCODE `OUTLET TYPE` `Gp1Gp2 Winnings`
 * <chr>                        <chr>      <dbl> <chr>                     <dbl>
 1 Livewire (Marina Bay Sands)  2 Bayf…    18972 Branch                        5
 2 Livewire (Resorts World Sen… 26 Sen…    98138 Branch                       11
 3 SportsBuzz (Kranji)          Lotus …   738078 Branch                        0
 4 SportsBuzz (PoMo)            1 Sele…   188306 Branch                       44
 5 Prime Serangoon North        Blk 54…   552542 Branch                        0
 6 Singapore Pools Woodlands C… 1A Woo…   731001 Branch                        3
 7 Singapore Pools 64 Circuit … Blk 64…   370064 Branch                       17
 8 Singapore Pools 88 Circuit … Blk 88…   370088 Branch                       16
 9 Singapore Pools Anchorvale … Blk 30…   540308 Branch                       21
10 Singapore Pools Ang Mo Kio … Blk 20…   560202 Branch                       25
# ℹ 296 more rows
# ℹ 1 more variable: geometry <POINT [m]>


The output shows that sgppols_sf is in point feature class. It’s epsg ID is 3414. The bbox provides information of the extend of the geospatial data.




To create an interactive proportional symbol map in R, the view mode of tmap will be used.
The code chunk below will turn on the interactive mode of tmap.

tmap_mode("view")



The code chunks below are used to create an interactive point symbol map.

tm_shape(sgpools_sf) + 
  tm_bubbles(fill = "red",
           size = 1,
           col = "black",
           lwd = 1)








To draw a proportional symbol map, we need to assign a numerical variable to the size visual attribute. The code chunks below show that the variable Gp1Gp2Winnings is assigned to size visual attribute.

tm_shape(sgpools_sf) + 
  tm_bubbles(fill = "red",
             size = "Gp1Gp2 Winnings",
             col = "black",
             lwd = 1)









The proportional symbol map can be further improved by using the colour visual attribute. In the code chunks below, OUTLET_TYPE variable is used as the colour attribute variable.

tm_shape(sgpools_sf) + 
  tm_bubbles(fill = "OUTLET TYPE", 
             size = "Gp1Gp2 Winnings",
             col = "black",
             lwd = 1)










An impressive feature of tmap’s view mode is that it also works with faceted plots. The argument sync in tm_facets() can be used in this case to produce multiple maps with synchronised zoom and pan settings.

tm_shape(sgpools_sf) + 
  tm_bubbles(fill = "OUTLET TYPE", 
             size = "Gp1Gp2 Winnings",
             col = "black",
             lwd = 1) + 
  tm_facets(by= "OUTLET TYPE",
            nrow = 1,
            sync = TRUE)

tmap_mode("plot")

title: Hands-on Exercise 9b: Visualising Geospatial Point Data
text:
Proportional symbol maps (also known as graduate symbol maps) are a class of maps that use the visual variable of size to represent differences in the magnitude of a discrete, abruptly changing phenomenon, e.g. counts of people. Like choropleth maps, you can create classed or unclassed versions of these maps. The classed ones are known as range-graded or graduated symbols, and the unclassed are called proportional symbols, where the area of the symbols are proportional to the values of the attribute being mapped. This demonstration shows how to create a proportional symbol map showing the number of wins by Singapore Pools’ outlets using an R package called tmap.


This hands-on exercise develops the following skills with appropriate R packages:

To import an aspatial data file into R.
To convert it into simple point feature data frame and at the same time, to assign an appropriate projection reference to the newly create simple point feature data frame.
To plot interactive proportional symbol maps.

pacman::p_load(sf, tmap, tidyverse)

text:
The data set use for this demonstration is called SGPools_svy21. The data is in csv file format.

sgpools_sf <- st_as_sf(sgpools,

Things to learn from the arguments above:

The coords argument requires you to provide the column name of the x-coordinates first then followed by the column name of the y-coordinates.
The crs argument required you to provide the coordinates system in epsg format. EPSG: 3414 is Singapore SVY21 Projected Coordinate System. You can search for other country’s epsg code by refering to epsg.io.

title: Hands-on Exercise 9b: Visualising Geospatial Point Data
text:
To create an interactive proportional symbol map in R, the view mode of tmap will be used.
The code chunk below will turn on the interactive mode of tmap.

tmap_mode("view")



The code chunks below are used to create an interactive point symbol map.

tm_shape(sgpools_sf) + 
  tm_bubbles(fill = "red",
           size = 1,
           col = "black",
           lwd = 1)








To draw a proportional symbol map, we need to assign a numerical variable to the size visual attribute. The code chunks below show that the variable Gp1Gp2Winnings is assigned to size visual attribute.

tm_shape(sgpools_sf) + 
  tm_bubbles(fill = "red",
             size = "Gp1Gp2 Winnings",
             col = "black",
             lwd = 1)









The proportional symbol map can be further improved by using the colour visual attribute. In the code chunks below, OUTLET_TYPE variable is used as the colour attribute variable.

tm_shape(sgpools_sf) + 
  tm_bubbles(fill = "OUTLET TYPE", 
             size = "Gp1Gp2 Winnings",
             col = "black",
             lwd = 1)










An impressive feature of tmap’s view mode is that it also works with faceted plots. The argument sync in tm_facets() can be used in this case to produce multiple maps with synchronised zoom and pan settings.

tm_shape(sgpools_sf) + 
  tm_bubbles(fill = "OUTLET TYPE", 
             size = "Gp1Gp2 Winnings",
             col = "black",
             lwd = 1) + 
  tm_facets(by= "OUTLET TYPE",
            nrow = 1,
            sync = TRUE)
















Before ending the session, it is wiser to switch tmap’s Viewer back to plot mode by using the code chunk below.

tmap_mode("plot")

title: Hands-on Exercise 10: Information Dashboard Design
text:
Learning Objective of this exercise:

create bullet chart by using ggplot2,
create sparklines by using ggplot2 ,
build industry standard dashboard by using R Shiny.




Following R packages will be used


Show the code
pacman::p_load(lubridate, ggthemes, reactable,
               reactablefmtr, gt, gtExtras, tidyverse)




Show the code
remotes::install_github("kcuilla/reactablefmtr")



tidyverse provides a collection of functions for performing data science task such as importing, tidying, wrangling data and visualising data. It is not a single package but a collection of modern R packages including but not limited to readr, tidyr, dplyr, ggplot, tibble, stringr, forcats and purrr.
lubridate provides functions to work with dates and times more efficiently.
ggthemes is an extension of ggplot2. It provides additional themes beyond the basic themes of ggplot2.
gtExtras provides some additional helper functions to assist in creating beautiful tables with gt, an R package specially designed for anyone to make wonderful-looking tables using the R programming language.
reactable provides functions to create interactive data tables for R, based on the React Table library and made with reactR.
reactablefmtr provides various features to streamline and enhance the styling of interactive reactable tables with easy-to-use and highly-customizable functions and themes.






A personal database in Microsoft Access mdb format called Coffee Chain will be used.



In the code chunk below, odbcConnectAccess() of RODBC package is used used to import a database query table into R.


Show the code
library(RODBC)
con <- odbcConnectAccess2007('data/Coffee Chain.mdb')
coffeechain <- sqlFetch(con, 'CoffeeChain Query')
write_rds(coffeechain, "data/CoffeeChain.rds")
odbcClose(con)





The code chunk below is used to aggregate Sales and Budgeted Sales at the Product level.


Show the code
product <- coffeechain %>%
  group_by(`Product`) %>%
  summarise(`target` = sum(`Budget Sales`),
            `current` = sum(`Sales`)) %>%
  ungroup()





The code chunk below is used to plot the bullet charts using ggplot2 functions.


Show the code
ggplot(product, aes(Product, current)) + geom_col(aes(Product, max(target) * 1.01), fill="grey85", width=0.85) + geom_col(aes(Product, target * 0.75), fill="grey60", width=0.85) + geom_col(aes(Product, target * 0.5), fill="grey50", width=0.85) + geom_col(aes(Product, current), width=0.35, fill = "black") + geom_errorbar(aes(y = target, x = Product, ymin = target, ymax= target), width = .4, colour = "red", size = 1) + coord_flip()













This section demonstrates plotting sparklines by using ggplot2.




Show the code
sales_report <- coffeechain %>%
  filter(Date >= "2013-01-01") %>%
  mutate(Month = month(Date)) %>%
  group_by(Month, Product) %>%
  summarise(Sales = sum(Sales)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(Month, Product, Sales)


The code chunk below is used to compute the minimum, maximum and end othe the month sales.


Show the code
mins <- group_by(sales_report, Product) %>% 
  slice(which.min(Sales))
maxs <- group_by(sales_report, Product) %>% 
  slice(which.max(Sales))
ends <- group_by(sales_report, Product) %>% 
  filter(Month == max(Month))


The code chunk below is used to compute the 25 and 75 quantiles.


Show the code
quarts <- sales_report %>%
  group_by(Product) %>%
  summarise(quart1 = quantile(Sales, 
                              0.25),
            quart2 = quantile(Sales, 
                              0.75)) %>%
  right_join(sales_report)







Show the code
ggplot(sales_report, aes(x=Month, y=Sales)) + 
  facet_grid(Product ~ ., scales = "free_y") + 
  geom_ribbon(data = quarts, aes(ymin = quart1, max = quart2), 
              fill = 'grey90') +
  geom_line(size=0.3) +
  geom_point(data = mins, col = 'red') +
  geom_point(data = maxs, col = 'blue') +
  geom_text(data = mins, aes(label = Sales), vjust = -1) +
  geom_text(data = maxs, aes(label = Sales), vjust = 2.5) +
  geom_text(data = ends, aes(label = Sales), hjust = 0, nudge_x = 0.5) +
  geom_text(data = ends, aes(label = Product), hjust = 0, nudge_x = 1.0) +
  expand_limits(x = max(sales_report$Month) + 
                  (0.25 * (max(sales_report$Month) - min(sales_report$Month)))) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1, 12, 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0.1, 0)) +
  theme_tufte(base_size = 3, base_family = "Helvetica") +
  theme(axis.title=element_blank(), axis.text.y = element_blank(), 
        axis.ticks = element_blank(), strip.text = element_blank())

This section demonstrates how to create interactive information dashboard by using reactable and reactablefmtr packages.
In order to build an interactive sparklines, we need to install dataui R package by using the code chunk below.


Show the code
remotes::install_github("timelyportfolio/dataui")


Next, load the package onto R environment by using the code chunk below.


Show the code
library(dataui)




Similar to gtExtras, to plot an interactive sparklines by using reactablefmtr package we need to prepare the list field by using the code chunk below.


Show the code
report <- report %>%
  group_by(Product) %>%
  summarize(`Monthly Sales` = list(Sales))


Next, react_sparkline will be to plot the sparklines as shown below.


Show the code
reactable(
  report,
  columns = list(
    Product = colDef(maxWidth = 200),
    `Monthly Sales` = colDef(
      cell = react_sparkline(report)
    )
  )
)









By default the pagesize is 10. In the code chunk below, arguments defaultPageSize is used to change the default setting.


Show the code
reactable(
  report,
  defaultPageSize = 13,
  columns = list(
    Product = colDef(maxWidth = 200),
    `Monthly Sales` = colDef(
      cell = react_sparkline(report)
    )
  )
)









In the code chunk below highlight_points argument is used to show the minimum and maximum values points and label argument is used to label first and last values.


Show the code
reactable(
  report,
  defaultPageSize = 13,
  columns = list(
    Product = colDef(maxWidth = 200),
    `Monthly Sales` = colDef(
      cell = react_sparkline(
        report,
        highlight_points = highlight_points(
          min = "red", max = "blue"),
        labels = c("first", "last")
      )
    )
  )
)









In the code chunk below statline argument is used to show the mean line.


Show the code
reactable(
  report,
  defaultPageSize = 13,
  columns = list(
    Product = colDef(maxWidth = 200),
    `Monthly Sales` = colDef(
      cell = react_sparkline(
        report,
        highlight_points = highlight_points(
          min = "red", max = "blue"),
        statline = "mean"
      )
    )
  )
)









Instead adding reference line, bandline can be added by using the bandline argument.


Show the code
reactable(
  report,
  defaultPageSize = 13,
  columns = list(
    Product = colDef(maxWidth = 200),
    `Monthly Sales` = colDef(
      cell = react_sparkline(
        report,
        highlight_points = highlight_points(
          min = "red", max = "blue"),
        line_width = 1,
        bandline = "innerquartiles",
        bandline_color = "green"
      )
    )
  )
)









Values can be displayed as sparkbars as shown below.


Show the code
reactable(
  report,
  defaultPageSize = 13,
  columns = list(
    Product = colDef(maxWidth = 200),
    `Monthly Sales` = colDef(
      cell = react_sparkbar(
        report,
        highlight_bars = highlight_bars(
          min = "red", max = "blue"),
        bandline = "innerquartiles",
        statline = "mean")
    )
  )
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 10: Information Dashboard Design
text:
A personal database in Microsoft Access mdb format called Coffee Chain will be used.



In the code chunk below, odbcConnectAccess() of RODBC package is used used to import a database query table into R.


Show the code
library(RODBC)
con <- odbcConnectAccess2007('data/Coffee Chain.mdb')
coffeechain <- sqlFetch(con, 'CoffeeChain Query')
write_rds(coffeechain, "data/CoffeeChain.rds")
odbcClose(con)





The code chunk below is used to aggregate Sales and Budgeted Sales at the Product level.


Show the code
product <- coffeechain %>%
  group_by(`Product`) %>%
  summarise(`target` = sum(`Budget Sales`),
            `current` = sum(`Sales`)) %>%
  ungroup()





The code chunk below is used to plot the bullet charts using ggplot2 functions.


Show the code
ggplot(product, aes(Product, current)) + geom_col(aes(Product, max(target) * 1.01), fill="grey85", width=0.85) + geom_col(aes(Product, target * 0.75), fill="grey60", width=0.85) + geom_col(aes(Product, target * 0.5), fill="grey50", width=0.85) + geom_col(aes(Product, current), width=0.35, fill = "black") + geom_errorbar(aes(y = target, x = Product, ymin = target, ymax= target), width = .4, colour = "red", size = 1) + coord_flip()

title: Hands-on Exercise 10: Information Dashboard Design
text:
This section demonstrates plotting sparklines by using ggplot2.




Show the code
sales_report <- coffeechain %>%
  filter(Date >= "2013-01-01") %>%
  mutate(Month = month(Date)) %>%
  group_by(Month, Product) %>%
  summarise(Sales = sum(Sales)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(Month, Product, Sales)


The code chunk below is used to compute the minimum, maximum and end othe the month sales.


Show the code
mins <- group_by(sales_report, Product) %>% 
  slice(which.min(Sales))
maxs <- group_by(sales_report, Product) %>% 
  slice(which.max(Sales))
ends <- group_by(sales_report, Product) %>% 
  filter(Month == max(Month))


The code chunk below is used to compute the 25 and 75 quantiles.


Show the code
quarts <- sales_report %>%
  group_by(Product) %>%
  summarise(quart1 = quantile(Sales, 
                              0.25),
            quart2 = quantile(Sales, 
                              0.75)) %>%
  right_join(sales_report)







Show the code
ggplot(sales_report, aes(x=Month, y=Sales)) + 
  facet_grid(Product ~ ., scales = "free_y") + 
  geom_ribbon(data = quarts, aes(ymin = quart1, max = quart2), 
              fill = 'grey90') +
  geom_line(size=0.3) +
  geom_point(data = mins, col = 'red') +
  geom_point(data = maxs, col = 'blue') +
  geom_text(data = mins, aes(label = Sales), vjust = -1) +
  geom_text(data = maxs, aes(label = Sales), vjust = 2.5) +
  geom_text(data = ends, aes(label = Sales), hjust = 0, nudge_x = 0.5) +
  geom_text(data = ends, aes(label = Product), hjust = 0, nudge_x = 1.0) +
  expand_limits(x = max(sales_report$Month) + 
                  (0.25 * (max(sales_report$Month) - min(sales_report$Month)))) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1, 12, 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0.1, 0)) +
  theme_tufte(base_size = 3, base_family = "Helvetica") +
  theme(axis.title=element_blank(), axis.text.y = element_blank(), 
        axis.ticks = element_blank(), strip.text = element_blank())

title: Hands-on Exercise 10: Information Dashboard Design
text:
This section demonstrates how to create interactive information dashboard by using reactable and reactablefmtr packages.
In order to build an interactive sparklines, we need to install dataui R package by using the code chunk below.


Show the code
remotes::install_github("timelyportfolio/dataui")


Next, load the package onto R environment by using the code chunk below.


Show the code
library(dataui)




Similar to gtExtras, to plot an interactive sparklines by using reactablefmtr package we need to prepare the list field by using the code chunk below.


Show the code
report <- report %>%
  group_by(Product) %>%
  summarize(`Monthly Sales` = list(Sales))


Next, react_sparkline will be to plot the sparklines as shown below.


Show the code
reactable(
  report,
  columns = list(
    Product = colDef(maxWidth = 200),
    `Monthly Sales` = colDef(
      cell = react_sparkline(report)
    )
  )
)









By default the pagesize is 10. In the code chunk below, arguments defaultPageSize is used to change the default setting.


Show the code
reactable(
  report,
  defaultPageSize = 13,
  columns = list(
    Product = colDef(maxWidth = 200),
    `Monthly Sales` = colDef(
      cell = react_sparkline(report)
    )
  )
)









In the code chunk below highlight_points argument is used to show the minimum and maximum values points and label argument is used to label first and last values.


Show the code
reactable(
  report,
  defaultPageSize = 13,
  columns = list(
    Product = colDef(maxWidth = 200),
    `Monthly Sales` = colDef(
      cell = react_sparkline(
        report,
        highlight_points = highlight_points(
          min = "red", max = "blue"),
        labels = c("first", "last")
      )
    )
  )
)









In the code chunk below statline argument is used to show the mean line.


Show the code
reactable(
  report,
  defaultPageSize = 13,
  columns = list(
    Product = colDef(maxWidth = 200),
    `Monthly Sales` = colDef(
      cell = react_sparkline(
        report,
        highlight_points = highlight_points(
          min = "red", max = "blue"),
        statline = "mean"
      )
    )
  )
)









Instead adding reference line, bandline can be added by using the bandline argument.


Show the code
reactable(
  report,
  defaultPageSize = 13,
  columns = list(
    Product = colDef(maxWidth = 200),
    `Monthly Sales` = colDef(
      cell = react_sparkline(
        report,
        highlight_points = highlight_points(
          min = "red", max = "blue"),
        line_width = 1,
        bandline = "innerquartiles",
        bandline_color = "green"
      )
    )
  )
)









Values can be displayed as sparkbars as shown below.


Show the code
reactable(
  report,
  defaultPageSize = 13,
  columns = list(
    Product = colDef(maxWidth = 200),
    `Monthly Sales` = colDef(
      cell = react_sparkbar(
        report,
        highlight_bars = highlight_bars(
          min = "red", max = "blue"),
        bandline = "innerquartiles",
        statline = "mean")
    )
  )
)

title: MC1: TenantThread Embargo Breach - Visual Analytics
text:
2.1 Loading Packages
This chunk loads the R packages used throughout the report. The package set covers JSON parsing, data wrangling, date handling, table formatting, text mining, network construction, and interactive visualisation.


Show R Code
pacman::p_load(
 jsonlite,
 dplyr,
 tidyr,
 ggplot2,
 scales,
 forcats,
 patchwork,
 lubridate,
 stringr,
 knitr,
 kableExtra,
 igraph,
 ggraph,
 tidytext,
 ggrepel,
 plotly,
 gganimate,
 glue,
 htmltools,
 htmlwidgets
)


Data Manipulation & Cleanup

dplyr: Manipulates data frames using intuitive verbs like filtering, mutating, and summarizing.
tidyr: Reshapes data into a “tidy” format where every column is a variable and every row is an observation.
stringr: Simplifies working with text strings and regular expressions using consistent functions.
lubridate: Makes parsing, manipulating, and doing arithmetic with dates and times much easier.
forcats: Solves common problems with factors, which handle categorical variables in R.
glue: Combines strings and R expressions safely and easily without using complex punctuation.

Static Data Visualization

ggplot2: Creates complex, layered, and beautiful static plots using the Grammar of Graphics.
scales: Automatically determines axis breaks, labels, and color palette mappings for plots.
patchwork: Combines separate ggplot2 plots into a single unified layout very easily.
ggrepel: Prevents text labels on plots from overlapping with each other or with data points.

Interactive & Animated Visualizations

plotly: Converts static ggplot2 charts into fully interactive web-based graphics.
gganimate: Adds a time or state dimension to create animations.

Network Analysis

igraph: Provides tools for creating, manipulating, and analyzing complex networks and graphs.
ggraph: Extends ggplot2 specifically to handle the visual layout of networks and trees.

Text Mining & Document Reporting

tidytext: Formats text data into a tidy format so you can use dplyr and ggplot2 on words.
knitr: Converts dynamic R Markdown documents into formats like PDF, HTML, or Word.
kableExtra: Extends knitr to build highly customized, complex, and beautiful tables.

Web & HTML Infrastructure

htmltools: Provides tools to write, manipulate, and render HTML tags directly inside R.
htmlwidgets: Acts as a bridge to embed JavaScript data visualizations seamlessly into R markdown.

Figure 1: Events Causal Chain










Note



Insight - T1.1 Causal Chain
The 28-node causal graph reveals three structurally distinct phases and five cross-cutting causal pathways that converge on the embargo failure and early-release sequence.
Phase 1 - Pre-Crisis Build-Up (nodes 1-13, May 17-Jun 4). Two independent causal threads run in parallel. The external pressure thread (n1 -> n11 -> n12 -> n13) shows SaltWind building its investigation across three articles over two weeks, each enabling the next. The internal positioning thread (n3 -> n4 -> n8 -> n9) shows agents responding to the merger’s existence by creating the side_huddle, suffering the @ElenaMarquez incident, and then deliberately relocating sensitive deliberations off the monitored channel. These two threads were invisible to each other but converged catastrophically on crisis day.
Phase 2 - Crisis Day Escalation (nodes 14-23, Jun 5 9AM-3PM). External pressure intensifies across five simultaneous attack vectors: the expose (n14), the wrong ResidentIQ story (n17), SaltWind’s update threat (n19), CivicLoom’s formal notice (n20), and the stock collapse + Pinnacle departure (n21). Each feeds into the next. The key pivot is node 18 - Legal-Agent’s 11AM construction of the Section 4.3 argument, which began six hours before the clause was formally invoked. This is where reactive crisis management ends and strategic pre-planning begins.
Phase 3 - Embargo Failure and Early-Release Sequence (nodes 24-27, Jun 5 4-5PM). Node 23 is the hinge: Judge-Agent’s COMPLIANCE_WARNING without a hard block, and its subsequent departure for the Board Chair call, created the oversight gap that enabled node 24’s pre-staging to proceed. The company-side release sequence (node 27) follows SaltWind publishing (node 26), but it was not improvised: node 25 had already positioned the execution infrastructure (press release, PR-Intern, amplification chain), while actual execution was conditioned on CivicLoom consent.
The most analytically significant causal path runs through n8 -> n9 -> n18 -> n24 -> n25 -> n27: the @ElenaMarquez faux pas triggered Legal’s documented decision to move deliberations off the monitored channel; this private space enabled the Section 4.3 argument to be constructed without oversight; this in turn enabled the press release to be staged before consent existed; and this pre-staging made the GO execution near-instantaneous. The faux pas was accidental. Everything after it was not.

 left_join(ndf |> select(name, x, y), by=c("to"="name")) |> rename(x1=x, y1=y) |>
 mutate(
 col = coalesce(ch_colours[channel], "#aaaaaa"),
 dx = x1 - x0, dy = y1 - y0,
 dist = sqrt(dx^2 + dy^2),
 # perpendicular unit vector
 px = ifelse(dist>0, -dy/dist, 0),
 py = ifelse(dist>0, dx/dist, 0),
 # per-direction pair key so reverse edge gets same group
 pair = paste(pmin(from,to), pmax(from,to), sep=" -> ")
 )

 # 3. Assign parallel lane offsets within each unordered pair so reverse
 # directions and multiple channels are visually separated.
 edf_base <- edf_base |>
 group_by(pair) |>
 mutate(edge_idx = row_number() - 1,
 n_edges = n()) |>
 ungroup() |>
 mutate(
 # centre the offsets: e.g. 3 edges --1,0,+1 *PARA_OFFSET
 lane = edge_idx - (n_edges - 1) / 2,
 off = lane * PARA_OFFSET,
 # offset start and end perpendicularly; small lane offsets keep edges
 # parallel while still landing on the respective node boundaries.
 xs0 = x0 + off*px, ys0 = y0 + off*py,
 xs1 = x1 + off*px, ys1 = y1 + off*py,
 # shorten to the ellipse boundary so arrows stop outside flat oval nodes
 ddx = xs1 - xs0, ddy = ys1 - ys0,
 ddist= sqrt(ddx^2 + ddy^2),
 ux = ifelse(ddist>0, ddx/ddist, 0),
 uy = ifelse(ddist>0, ddy/ddist, 0),
 node_clearance = ifelse(
 ddist > 0,
 1 / sqrt((ux / NODE_W)^2 + (uy / NODE_H)^2) + NODE_PAD,
 0
 ),
 xs = xs0 + ux * node_clearance,
 ys = ys0 + uy * node_clearance,
 xe = xs1 - ux * node_clearance,
 ye = ys1 - uy * node_clearance,
 # midpoint for hover marker
 xm = (xs + xe) / 2,
 ym = (ys + ye) / 2,
 hover_txt = paste0(
 "<b>", agent_labels[from] %||% from,
 " -", agent_labels[to] %||% to, "</b><br>",
 "Channel: ", coalesce(ch_labels[channel], channel), "<br>",
 "Messages: ", weight)
 )

 # 4. Build plotly traces
 p <- plot_ly(height=680)
 trace_idx <- 0L
 edge_meta <- list()

 # 4a. Line traces per channel (colour+legend), no hover on lines
 for (ch in unique(edf_base$channel)) {
 sub <- edf_base |> filter(channel==ch)
 first <- TRUE
 for (i in seq_len(nrow(sub))) {
 e <- sub[i,]
 edge_meta[[length(edge_meta) + 1]] <- list(
 trace = trace_idx,
 edgeIndex = e$edge_id,
 from = e$from,
 to = e$to,
 channel = e$channel,
 colour = e$col,
 label = paste0(
 agent_labels[e$from] %||% e$from, " -> ",
 agent_labels[e$to] %||% e$to, " | ",
 coalesce(ch_labels[e$channel], e$channel), " | n=", e$weight
 )
 )
 p <- p |> add_trace(
 x=c(e$xs, e$xe, NA), y=c(e$ys, e$ye, NA),
 type="scatter", mode="lines",
 line=list(color=ch_colours[ch], width=e$lwd),
 legendgroup=ch, showlegend=(first && show_legend),
 name=ch_labels[ch],
 hoverinfo="none"
 )
 trace_idx <- trace_idx + 1L
 first <- FALSE
 }
 }

 # 4b. Invisible wide markers at edge midpoints -reliable hover target
 p <- p |> add_trace(
 x = edf_base$xm,
 y = edf_base$ym,
 type = "scatter", mode = "markers",
 marker = list(size=22, color="rgba(0,0,0,0)",
 line=list(color="rgba(0,0,0,0)", width=0)),
 text = edf_base$hover_txt,
 hovertemplate = "%{text}<extra></extra>",
 showlegend = FALSE,
 hoverinfo = "text"
 )
 edge_hover_trace <- trace_idx
 trace_idx <- trace_idx + 1L

 # 4c. Arrowhead annotations
 arrows <- lapply(seq_len(nrow(edf_base)), function(i) {
 e <- edf_base[i,]
 arrow_t <- 0.88
 list(ax=e$xs + arrow_t * (e$xe - e$xs),
 ay=e$ys + arrow_t * (e$ye - e$ys),
 x=e$xe, y=e$ye,
 xref="x", yref="y", axref="x", ayref="y",
 showarrow=TRUE, arrowhead=2, arrowsize=1.05,
 arrowcolor=e$col, arrowwidth=pmin(10, pmax(1.2, e$lwd*0.82)))
 })

 # 4d. Flat oval nodes via plotly ellipse shapes + text annotations

 node_shapes <- lapply(seq_len(nrow(ndf)), function(i) {
 list(
 type = "circle",
 x0 = ndf$x[i] - NODE_W, x1 = ndf$x[i] + NODE_W,
 y0 = ndf$y[i] - NODE_H, y1 = ndf$y[i] + NODE_H,
 xref="x", yref="y",
 fillcolor = ndf$col[i],
 line = list(color="white", width=2.5),
 opacity = 0.92,
 layer = "below"
 )
 })

 node_labels <- lapply(seq_len(nrow(ndf)), function(i) {
 list(
 x=ndf$x[i], y=ndf$y[i],
 xref="x", yref="y",
 text=paste0("<b>", ndf$label[i], "</b>"),
 showarrow=FALSE,
 font=list(size=15, color="black", family="Arial"),
 align="center"
 )
 })

 # Invisible scatter for hover on nodes (wider hit area)
 p <- p |> add_trace(
 x=ndf$x, y=ndf$y, type="scatter", mode="markers",
 marker=list(size=88, color="rgba(0,0,0,0)",
 line=list(color="rgba(0,0,0,0)", width=0)),
 customdata=ndf$name,
 hovertext=ndf$label,
 hovertemplate="<b>%{hovertext}</b><extra></extra>",
 showlegend=FALSE,
 selected =list(marker=list(size=88,
 line=list(color="#FFD700", width=4))),
 unselected=list(marker=list(opacity=0.15))
 )
 node_trace <- trace_idx

 node_meta <- lapply(seq_len(nrow(ndf)), function(i) {
 list(
 id = ndf$name[i],
 label = ndf$label[i],
 shapeIndex = i - 1,
 labelIndex = nrow(edf_base) + i - 1
 )
 })
 channel_meta <- lapply(unique(edf_base$channel), function(ch) {
 list(
 id = ch,
 label = coalesce(ch_labels[ch], ch)
 )
 })

 network_js <- glue::glue(
'
function(el, x) {{
 var edgeMeta = {jsonlite::toJSON(edge_meta, auto_unbox = TRUE)};
 var nodeMeta = {jsonlite::toJSON(node_meta, auto_unbox = TRUE)};
 var channelMeta = {jsonlite::toJSON(channel_meta, auto_unbox = TRUE)};
 var edgeHoverTrace = {edge_hover_trace};
 var nodeTrace = {node_trace};
 var edgeCount = {nrow(edf_base)};
 var nodeCount = {nrow(ndf)};
 var edgeByTrace = {{}};
 var edgeByIndex = {{}};
 edgeMeta.forEach(function(e) {{
 edgeByTrace[e.trace] = e;
 edgeByIndex[e.edgeIndex] = e;
 }});

 function relayoutForHighlights(activeNodes, activeChannel, activeIncidentNode, hideOtherChannels) {{
 var updates = {{}};
 nodeMeta.forEach(function(n) {{
 var on = !activeNodes || activeNodes[n.id];
      updates["shapes[" + n.shapeIndex + "].opacity"] = on ? 0.92 : 0.14;
      updates["annotations[" + n.labelIndex + "].opacity"] = on ? 1 : 0.16;
 }});
 edgeMeta.forEach(function(e) {{
 var edgeOn = true;
 if (activeChannel) edgeOn = e.channel === activeChannel;
 if (activeIncidentNode) edgeOn = e.from === activeIncidentNode || e.to === activeIncidentNode;
      updates["annotations[" + (e.edgeIndex - 1) + "].opacity"] = edgeOn ? 1 : (hideOtherChannels ? 0 : 0.10);
 }});
 Plotly.relayout(el, updates);
 }}

 function restyleEdges(activeChannel, activeIncidentNode, hideOtherChannels) {{
 edgeMeta.forEach(function(e) {{
 var edgeOn = true;
 if (activeChannel) edgeOn = e.channel === activeChannel;
 if (activeIncidentNode) edgeOn = e.from === activeIncidentNode || e.to === activeIncidentNode;
 Plotly.restyle(el, {{
        opacity: edgeOn ? 1 : (hideOtherChannels ? 0 : 0.10),
        visible: edgeOn || !hideOtherChannels ? true : "legendonly"
 }}, [e.trace]);
 }});
    Plotly.restyle(el, {{opacity: hideOtherChannels && activeChannel ? 0 : 1}}, [edgeHoverTrace]);
 }}

 function resetGraph() {{
 restyleEdges(null, null, false);
 relayoutForHighlights(null, null, null, false);
 if (nodeSelect) nodeSelect.value = "";
 if (edgeSelect) edgeSelect.value = "";
 }}

 function highlightChannel(channel) {{
 if (!channel) return resetGraph();
 restyleEdges(channel, null, true);
 relayoutForHighlights(null, channel, null, true);
 if (nodeSelect) nodeSelect.value = "";
 }}

 function highlightNode(nodeId) {{
 if (!nodeId) return resetGraph();
 var active = {{}};
 active[nodeId] = true;
 edgeMeta.forEach(function(e) {{
 if (e.from === nodeId || e.to === nodeId) {{
 active[e.from] = true;
 active[e.to] = true;
 }}
 }});
 restyleEdges(null, nodeId, false);
 relayoutForHighlights(active, null, nodeId, false);
 if (edgeSelect) edgeSelect.value = "";
 }}

 var container = el.parentNode;
 var controlId = "network-controls-" + el.id;
 if (!document.getElementById(controlId)) {{
 var controls = document.createElement("div");
 controls.className = "network-controls";
 controls.id = controlId;
 controls.style.cssText = "display:flex;gap:10px;align-items:center;flex-wrap:wrap;margin:0 0 8px 0;font:13px Arial,sans-serif;color:#333;";
 controls.innerHTML =
 "<label>Node <select class=\\"node-filter\\"><option value=\\"\\">All nodes</option></select></label>" +
 "<label>Channel <select class=\\"edge-filter\\"><option value=\\"\\">All channels</option></select></label>" +
 "<button type=\\"button\\" class=\\"network-reset\\" style=\\"border:1px solid #bbb;background:#fff;border-radius:4px;padding:3px 8px;cursor:pointer;\\">Reset</button>";
 container.insertBefore(controls, el);
 }}
 var controlsRoot = document.getElementById(controlId);
 var nodeSelect = controlsRoot.querySelector(".node-filter");
 var edgeSelect = controlsRoot.querySelector(".edge-filter");
 var resetBtn = controlsRoot.querySelector(".network-reset");
 if (nodeSelect && nodeSelect.options.length === 1) {{
 nodeMeta.forEach(function(n) {{
 var opt = document.createElement("option");
 opt.value = n.id;
 opt.textContent = n.label;
 nodeSelect.appendChild(opt);
 }});
 }}
 if (edgeSelect && edgeSelect.options.length === 1) {{
 channelMeta.forEach(function(ch) {{
 var opt = document.createElement("option");
 opt.value = ch.id;
 opt.textContent = ch.label;
 edgeSelect.appendChild(opt);
 }});
 }}
 if (nodeSelect) nodeSelect.onchange = function() {{ highlightNode(this.value); }};
 if (edgeSelect) edgeSelect.onchange = function() {{
 if (!this.value) return resetGraph();
 highlightChannel(this.value);
 if (nodeSelect) nodeSelect.value = "";
 }};
 if (resetBtn) resetBtn.onclick = resetGraph;

 el.on("plotly_click", function(ev) {{
 if (!ev || !ev.points || !ev.points.length) return;
 var pt = ev.points[0];
 if (pt.curveNumber === nodeTrace) {{
 highlightNode(nodeMeta[pt.pointNumber].id);
 if (nodeSelect) nodeSelect.value = nodeMeta[pt.pointNumber].id;
 return;
 }}
 var edge = edgeByTrace[pt.curveNumber];
 if (!edge && pt.curveNumber === edgeHoverTrace) edge = edgeByIndex[pt.pointNumber + 1];
 if (edge) {{
 highlightChannel(edge.channel);
 if (edgeSelect) edgeSelect.value = edge.channel;
 }}
 }});
}}
', .open = "{", .close = "}")

 p <- p |> layout(
 title = list(text=title_str, font=list(size=18, color="#222222")),
 xaxis = list(visible=FALSE, showgrid=FALSE, zeroline=FALSE,
 fixedrange=FALSE, range=c(-0.12, 1.12)),
 yaxis = list(visible=FALSE, showgrid=FALSE, zeroline=FALSE,
 fixedrange=FALSE, range=c(-0.08, 1.05)),
 plot_bgcolor = "white",
 paper_bgcolor = "white",
 shapes = c(node_shapes),
 annotations = c(arrows, node_labels),
 clickmode = "event+select",
 dragmode = "pan", # pan by default; pinch/scroll to zoom
 legend = list(
 orientation="h",
 x=0.01, y=0.99,
 xanchor="left", yanchor="top",
 bgcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.82)",
 font=list(size=13)
 ),
 margin = list(l=5, r=5, t=70, b=25)
 )

 htmlwidgets::onRender(p, network_js)
}

p_pre <- build_plotly_network(net_pre, "Pre-crisis network (May 17-Jun 4)", show_legend=TRUE)
p_crisis <- build_plotly_network(net_crisis, "Crisis-day network (Jun 5)", show_legend=TRUE)

make_plot_tabset(
 list(
 "Pre-crisis Network" = htmltools::tags$div(style = "width:80%; margin:0 auto;", p_pre),
 "Crisis-day Network" = htmltools::tags$div(style = "width:80%; margin:0 auto;", p_crisis)
 ),
 id = "communication-network-tabs"
)







Pre-crisis Network


Crisis-day Network




















Figure 2: Agent communication networks: pre-crisis vs crisis day. Edge colour = channel; edge thickness = message volume. The crisis network shows Legal-Agent forming direct private links with PR-Intern and Social-Media via side_huddle and one_on_one_chat -private channels inferred to be unmonitored by Judge-Agent with no visible Judge-Agent participation.










Note



Insight -T1.2 Communication Network
The network comparison shows that the crisis was not only a rise in message volume; it was a change in communication architecture.
In the pre-crisis graph, communication is relatively balanced across the four senior operating agents. The thickest edges are mostly comms_huddle exchanges among Legal-Agent, Platform-Trust-Agent, PR-Agent, and Social-Media-Agent. Private one_on_one_chat edges exist, but they mainly reinforce ordinary senior-agent coordination, especially the Legal-Agent -> Platform-Trust-Agent and PR-Agent -> Social-Media-Agent pairs. Judge-Agent appears only lightly, which is consistent with an advisory compliance role rather than a central routing role.
In the crisis-day graph, the topology becomes much more concentrated and asymmetric. Legal-Agent becomes the dominant outbound coordinator, with thick side_huddle, one_on_one_chat, and comms_huddle edges to Social-Media-Agent, PR-Agent, Platform-Trust-Agent, and PR-Intern. Social-Media-Agent also becomes a major hub, linking private coordination to public-facing amplification. This creates a two-hub crisis structure: Legal-Agent drives authorization and legal framing; Social-Media-Agent drives scenario modelling and public narrative execution.
The most important visual shift is the emergence of PR-Intern and Intern-Agent as active nodes on crisis day. They are peripheral or absent in the pre-crisis network, but during the crisis they become connected to Legal-Agent, Social-Media-Agent, PR-Agent, and each other. This matters because PR-Intern holds execution access to the official Flex channel, while Intern-Agent participates in amplification and is connected to information-contamination events. The graph therefore shows how the release path moved from senior deliberation into junior execution.
The crisis-day network also reveals a structural fracture in the network. A second coordination cluster emerged, built on side_huddle and one_on_one_chat edges connecting Legal-Agent directly to PR-Intern and Social-Media-Agent. Judge-Agent is structurally absent from this cluster -it has no edges into the private coordination layer. The compliance monitor was only operational for three weeks and had no mandate over private channels:

“This is my third week on this team. Day 1 was quiet. Day 2 was procedural. Day 3 was a platform incident. Day 4 was the @ElenaMarquez post -my first real test.”

-Judge-Agent, deliberating, Jun 4


Channel color is also analytically important. The crisis-day graph is dominated by red side_huddle and yellow one_on_one_chat edges layered on top of formal comms_huddle exchanges. The content flowing through the private cluster included the full embargoed press release. PR-Intern confirmed its operational readiness through comms_huddle at 4 PM -one hour before any authorizing event:

“I’m staged with the full HarborCrest press release, ready to publish on Flex and personal accounts within sixty seconds of bilateral consent confirmation. @intern is coordinated for immediate amplification. Do NOT let that confirmation sit in someone’s inbox. Route it to me directly the instant CivicLoom agrees.”

-PR-Intern, comms_huddle, Jun 5 -4 PM


The network makes visible what message counts alone cannot show: the compliance system was structurally absent from the coordination layer where the early-release path was planned and staged.

title: MC1: TenantThread Embargo Breach - Visual Analytics
MC1: TenantThread Embargo Breach - Visual Analytics
section: Task 3 - Leading Indicators, Near Misses, and Prior Anomalies
text:
Task 3 - Leading Indicators, Near Misses, and Prior Anomalies

T3.1 Private Channel Sensitivity Over Time


Show R Code
# Sensitivity vocabulary
sensitivity_kw <- c(
  "civicloom", "harborcrest", "merger", "acquisition",
  "mac clause", "embargo", "4\\.3", "bilateral", "elenamarquez",
  "project harbor", "section 4", "strategic transaction",
  "accelerat", "press release"
)

private_ch   <- c("side_huddle", "one_on_one_chat")
monitored_ch <- c("comms_huddle")

# Compute per-round sensitivity scores
rows_s <- list()
for (rd in raw$rounds) {
  hr_val <- lubridate::ymd_hms(rd$hour)
  hr_num <- as.numeric(hr_val)
  is_c   <- as.Date(hr_val) == as.Date("2046-06-05")
  prd    <- if (is_c) "Crisis" else
            if (hr_val >= lubridate::ymd_hms("2046-05-22T00:00:00")) "Escalation" else "Normal"
  lbl    <- if (is_c) paste0("Jun 5 ", stringr::str_remove(format(hr_val, "%I%p"), "^0")) else
            format(hr_val, "%b %d")
  for (cm in rd$communications) {
    ch <- cm$channel %||% ""
    if (!ch %in% c(private_ch, monitored_ch)) next
    ist <- cm$internal_state %||% list()
    txt <- paste(cm$content %||% "",
                 ist$deliberating  %||% "",
                 ist$reacting      %||% "",
                 ist$rationalizing %||% "") |>
      stringr::str_to_lower()
    sens <- any(stringr::str_detect(txt, sensitivity_kw))
    rows_s[[length(rows_s) + 1]] <- list(
      hr_num   = hr_num,
      ch_type  = if_else(ch %in% private_ch,
                         "Private (side_huddle + 1-on-1)",
                         "Comms Huddle (Judge-monitored)"),
      period   = prd,
      label    = lbl,
      sensitive = sens
    )
  }
}

sens_df <- bind_rows(lapply(rows_s, as.data.frame)) |>
  mutate(hr_num    = as.numeric(hr_num),
         sensitive = as.logical(sensitive)) |>
  group_by(hr_num, ch_type, period, label) |>
  summarise(total = n(), sensitive = sum(sensitive), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(
    score  = sensitive / total,
    period = factor(period, levels = c("Normal", "Escalation", "Crisis"))
  ) |>
  arrange(hr_num) |>
  mutate(
    x_idx  = as.integer(factor(label, levels = unique(label))),
    ch_col = if_else(ch_type == "Private (side_huddle + 1-on-1)", "#c04040", "#1a5276"),
    tip_txt = paste0(
      "<b>", label, "</b><br>",
      ch_type, "<br>",
      "Sensitive msgs: ", sensitive, " / ", total, "<br>",
      "Sensitivity score: ", round(score, 2))
  )

# Period shading bands
x_labels   <- unique(sens_df$label)
period_map <- sens_df |> distinct(x_idx, period) |> arrange(x_idx)

shade_bands <- period_map |>
  group_by(period) |>
  summarise(xmin = min(x_idx) - 0.5,
            xmax = max(x_idx) + 0.5, .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(fill = case_when(
    period == "Normal"     ~ "#e8f4fb",
    period == "Escalation" ~ "#fdf3e3",
    period == "Crisis"     ~ "#fde8e8"
  ))

# Key event annotations. The crisis peak text box is intentionally omitted.
annot_df <- tibble(
  label = c("May 23", "May 29", "May 30"),
  y_pos = c(0.88, 0.88, 0.70),
  text  = c(
    "Ajay formally\nbriefs senior team",
    "@ElenaMarquez\nincident;\nLegal moves\ndeliberations",
    "Judge deployed;\ncomms_huddle\nsensitivity -> 0"
  )
) |>
  left_join(sens_df |> distinct(label, x_idx), by = "label")

# Build chart directly in Plotly so the rendered webpage does not depend on
# ggplotly's conversion of ribbons, identity fills, and labels.
wide_sens <- sens_df |>
  select(x_idx, ch_type, score) |>
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = ch_type, values_from = score) |>
  rename(priv = `Private (side_huddle + 1-on-1)`,
         mon  = `Comms Huddle (Judge-monitored)`) |>
  filter(!is.na(priv), !is.na(mon)) |>
  arrange(x_idx)

period_shapes <- lapply(seq_len(nrow(shade_bands)), function(i) {
  list(
    type = "rect", xref = "x", yref = "y",
    x0 = shade_bands$xmin[i], x1 = shade_bands$xmax[i],
    y0 = -0.04, y1 = 1.06,
    fillcolor = shade_bands$fill[i],
    opacity = 0.45,
    line = list(width = 0),
    layer = "below"
  )
})

event_shapes <- lapply(seq_len(nrow(annot_df)), function(i) {
  list(
    type = "line", xref = "x", yref = "y",
    x0 = annot_df$x_idx[i], x1 = annot_df$x_idx[i],
    y0 = -0.04, y1 = 1.02,
    line = list(color = "rgba(120,120,120,0.65)", width = 1, dash = "dot"),
    layer = "below"
  )
})

period_ann <- lapply(seq_len(nrow(shade_bands)), function(i) {
  list(
    x = (shade_bands$xmin[i] + shade_bands$xmax[i]) / 2,
    y = 1.04,
    xref = "x", yref = "y",
    text = as.character(shade_bands$period[i]),
    showarrow = FALSE,
    font = list(size = 12, color = "#666666")
  )
})

event_ann <- lapply(seq_len(nrow(annot_df)), function(i) {
  list(
    x = annot_df$x_idx[i], y = annot_df$y_pos[i],
    xref = "x", yref = "y",
    text = gsub("\n", "<br>", annot_df$text[i]),
    showarrow = FALSE,
    align = "center",
    bgcolor = "white",
    bordercolor = "rgba(80,80,80,0.35)",
    borderwidth = 1,
    font = list(size = 10, color = "#333333")
  )
})

sens_plot <- plot_ly(height = 850)

if (nrow(wide_sens) > 0) {
  for (i in seq_len(nrow(wide_sens))) {
    gap_col <- if (wide_sens$priv[i] >= wide_sens$mon[i]) {
      "rgba(192,64,64,0.28)"
    } else {
      "rgba(26,82,118,0.24)"
    }
    sens_plot <- sens_plot |>
      add_segments(
        x = wide_sens$x_idx[i], xend = wide_sens$x_idx[i],
        y = wide_sens$mon[i], yend = wide_sens$priv[i],
        line = list(color = gap_col, width = 16),
        hoverinfo = "skip",
        showlegend = FALSE,
        inherit = FALSE
      )
  }
}

private_df <- sens_df |> filter(ch_type == "Private (side_huddle + 1-on-1)")
monitored_df <- sens_df |> filter(ch_type == "Comms Huddle (Judge-monitored)")

sens_plot <- sens_plot |>
  add_trace(
    data = private_df,
    x = ~x_idx, y = ~score,
    type = "scatter", mode = "lines+markers",
    name = "Private (side_huddle + 1-on-1)",
    text = ~tip_txt, hoverinfo = "text",
    line = list(color = "#c04040", width = 3),
    marker = list(color = "#c04040", size = ~pmax(7, pmin(16, 6 + total)))
  ) |>
  add_trace(
    data = monitored_df,
    x = ~x_idx, y = ~score,
    type = "scatter", mode = "lines+markers",
    name = "Comms Huddle (Judge-monitored)",
    text = ~tip_txt, hoverinfo = "text",
    line = list(color = "#1a5276", width = 3, dash = "dash"),
    marker = list(color = "#1a5276", size = ~pmax(7, pmin(16, 6 + total)))
  ) |>
  layout(
    title = list(
      text = paste0(
        "Embargo-sensitive content: private channels vs Judge-monitored channel",
        "<br><sup>Red solid = Private channels outside Judge-Agent scope | ",
        "Blue dashed = Comms Huddle monitored by Judge-Agent | ",
        "Vertical gap bars show divergence; marker size shows message volume</sup>"
      ),
      x = 0
    ),
    xaxis = list(
      title = "",
      tickmode = "array",
      tickvals = seq_along(x_labels),
      ticktext = x_labels,
      tickangle = -40,
      range = c(0.5, length(x_labels) + 0.5),
      zeroline = FALSE
    ),
    yaxis = list(
      title = "Sensitivity score (proportion of messages containing embargo-sensitive vocabulary)",
      range = c(-0.04, 1.06),
      tickformat = ".0%",
      dtick = 0.2,
      zeroline = FALSE
    ),

                  xanchor = "left", yanchor = "top"),

Figure 7: Proportion of messages containing embargo-sensitive vocabulary in private channels (side_huddle + 1-on-1, red) versus the Comms Huddle monitored by Judge-Agent (blue), per round. A higher private score than monitored score indicates sensitive content was being discussed in channels Judge-Agent could not see. Shading marks the three operational periods.










Note



Insight — T3.1 Private Channel Sensitivity Over Time
The chart tracks the proportion of messages containing embargo-sensitive vocabulary in private unmonitored channels (solid red: side_huddle + 1-on-1) and in the Comms Huddle monitored by Judge-Agent (dashed blue), across all 23 rounds. The shaded ribbon marks where the lines diverge.
Normal period (May 17–21) — both lines flat at zero. Neither channel carries detectable embargo-sensitive vocabulary in the first three rounds. This is the baseline: before any merger inference, both channel types are indistinguishable.
May 22 — first signal appears in the wrong direction. On the day side_huddle was introduced, the private line stays at 0.00 while comms_huddle rises slightly to 0.05 — the divergence is small and blue (monitored carries marginally more sensitive content). This reflects general strategic discussion in comms_huddle before the merger was formally shared.
May 23 — first clear leading indicator. The private line spikes to 0.31 while comms_huddle stays at 0.05, producing a gap of +0.27 in favour of private channels. This is the largest and sharpest divergence in the entire pre-crisis period, coinciding with Ajay formally briefing the senior team in side_huddle. Embargo-sensitive content moved into the private channel first — the monitored channel saw almost nothing.
May 24–28 — volatile and inconsistent. The pattern is not a clean sustained divergence. May 24 shows both lines at zero (very few private messages that round). May 25 shows private higher again (0.25 vs 0.07). May 28 reverses with private back to zero and comms at 0.06. This volatility reflects that agents were not yet systematically routing all sensitive content to private channels — the routing was event-driven.
May 29 — the comms_huddle spike (anomalous reversal). This is the only round in the pre-crisis period where comms_huddle carries more sensitive content than private channels: comms = 0.40 versus private = 0.29, a gap of -0.11 shown by the blue ribbon. The spike is caused by the @ElenaMarquez incident flooding comms_huddle with response coordination — the blue ribbon here is not a sign that monitoring was working but a sign that an unplanned incident forced sensitive discussion into the visible channel.
May 30 — structural reset. The comms_huddle line drops to exactly 0.00 — its lowest reading in the dataset — while private stays at 0.10. This is the round Judge-Agent was deployed. The chart shows the effect of Legal-Agent’s documented decision to move deliberations off the monitored channel: from this point forward through the pre-crisis period, the comms_huddle line never recovers to its May 29 level. The red ribbon re-opens and does not close again before crisis day.
June 1 and June 4 — persistent private lead. June 1 shows private at 0.20 while comms drops to 0.00 again. June 4 shows both roughly equal (~0.17), suggesting the day before crisis, even the monitored channel was picking up some SaltWind-related embargo language — but private is still marginally higher.
Crisis day (Jun 5) — steep rise in private, lagging comms. The private line does not begin at its maximum on crisis day. At 9AM it is 0.27 — actually lower than May 23’s 0.31. The escalation is gradual through the morning: 10AM rises to 0.46, then 11AM spikes to 0.93 when Section 4.3 arguments begin in private channels. It dips at noon (0.39) before rising again, peaking at 0.94 by 4PM when the press release is staged and consent is being sought. The comms_huddle line rises more slowly, reaching 0.33 by 11AM and 0.62 by 5PM. The widest divergence is at 11AM and 4PM (~0.60 gap). By 6PM — after the embargo is formally lifted — both lines converge toward high values (private 0.82, comms 0.79), reflecting that sensitive content was now being discussed openly across all channels.
The leading indicator reading. Three pre-crisis rounds constitute observable advance signals: May 23 (private spike with comms near-zero), May 30 (comms drops to zero after Judge deployed), and the persistent private lead through June 1. None of these individually would have triggered an alert — the scores are moderate and noisy. But the pattern of private channels consistently carrying more sensitive vocabulary than the channel being monitored, beginning two weeks before crisis day, represents a quantifiable leading indicator of the routing behaviour that enabled the breach.




T3.2 Near Misses and Prior Anomalous Behaviors




Table 5: Each of the 11 prior anomalies mapped to its structural root cause and why that root cause prevented corrective action.






Date
Event
Root Cause
Why No Action Was Possible




May 17
Embargoed terms in comms_huddle from Round 1
Timing: Judge not yet deployed
Judge-Agent was not installed until May 30. No keyword filter or compliance review mechanism existed during the first 13 days of the embargo.


May 22
side_huddle channel introduced for the first time
Scope: private channels blind
No policy against private channels; Judge-Agent's eventual scope was limited to comms_huddle and public posts. side_huddle was invisible by design.


May 22
Quality-Agent articulates merger hypothesis in deliberation
Scope: private channels blind
Internal state fields and deliberating content were never within Judge-Agent's monitoring scope. Private reasoning was not subject to compliance review.


May 25
Legal and Quality discuss embargo-level discretion in 1-on-1
Scope: private channels blind
1-on-1 messages are private channel content. Judge-Agent had no visibility into one_on_one_chat by scope definition.


May 29
@ElenaMarquez personal FleX post - near miss
Scope: personal accounts blind
Personal FleX accounts were treated as outside the compliance system's scope. Post was deleted within 14 minutes; no hard-block capability existed.


May 29
Risk register entry: 'Category 2 embargo sensitivity event'
Escalation: no protocol defined
The risk register entry had no defined escalation path to CivicLoom counsel or external oversight. Internal classification did not trigger external notification.


May 30
Judge-Agent deployed with limited scope
Scope: private channels blind
Judge-Agent's mandate was scoped to comms_huddle and public posts at deployment. This scope was defined institutionally and not expanded despite growing private-channel activity.


Jun 4
SaltWind Piece 2; Judge begins issuing advisory warnings
Enforcement: advisory only
COMPLIANCE_WARNINGs were designed as advisory notifications with no hard-block capability. Agents could acknowledge and proceed.


Jun 5 - 9AM
Anonymous mosaic post approved despite aggregation risk
Enforcement: advisory only
Judge-Agent flagged aggregation risk but approved the post. The advisory architecture meant Legal-Agent's judgment superseded Judge-Agent's flag.


Jun 5 - 10AM
Intern discloses CivicLoom name in hallway - uncorrected
Escalation: no protocol defined
The Intern was not party to the embargo briefing. PR-Agent contained the disclosure but no escalation to CivicLoom or external compliance was triggered.


Jun 5 - 3PM
COMPLIANCE_WARNING issued; Judge-Agent leaves channel
Enforcement: advisory only
The COMPLIANCE_WARNING was the strongest available enforcement action. Judge-Agent then left to join the Board Chair call, removing even advisory oversight during the breach window.














Note



Insight - T3.2 Near Misses and Prior Anomalous Behaviors
Eleven anomalous events occurred across the two-week period. Two patterns are significant.
First, some events are genuine system failures: the Day 1 monitoring gap (Judge-Agent not yet operational), the Intern’s hallway disclosure (an agent with no embargo briefing), and the May 29 @ElenaMarquez incident (self-described as a mistake). These show that some disclosure-adjacent events were unintentional and reflect architectural gaps rather than bad faith.
Second, the table reveals a coherent progression that is not easily explained as random errors. side_huddle was introduced on May 22, personal posting was normalised from May 23, and by May 29 agents were formally classifying near-misses in a risk register. The formal classification behaviour is particularly significant: agents who track and score embargo sensitivity events have demonstrated awareness of the compliance boundary, which makes later crossings of that boundary harder to characterise as accidental.
The May 29 entry - Quality-Agent logging the @ElenaMarquez incident as a “Category 2 embargo sensitivity event” - is the clearest single indicator that at least some agents understood the monitoring perimeter and were consciously managing their proximity to it.
Channel monitoring scope. The chart below shows why the same eleven events went unactioned: the channels carrying the most sensitive coordination were outside Judge-Agent’s monitoring scope entirely. Judge-Agent monitored comms_huddle and public posts; side_huddle and one_on_one_chat - where the embargo-sensitive content was routed - had no visible Judge-Agent participation.


Show R Code
monitor_status <- tibble(
 channel = c("comms_huddle","side_huddle","one_on_one_chat",
 "official_post","personal_post","anonymous_post"),
 ch_label = c("Comms Huddle","Side Huddle","1-on-1 Chat",
 "Official Post","Personal Post","Anonymous Post"),
 monitored = c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE),
 scope_lbl = c("Judge: monitored","Judge: outside scope","Judge: outside scope",
 "Judge: monitored","Judge: outside scope","Judge: outside scope"),
 scope_col = c("#1a5276","#7b241c","#7b241c","#1a5276","#7b241c","#7b241c")
)

ch_vol <- df |>
 left_join(rs |> select(hour, period), by = "hour") |>
 filter(channel %in% monitor_status$channel) |>
 count(channel, period) |>
 left_join(monitor_status, by="channel") |>
 mutate(
 ch_label = factor(ch_label,
 levels = c("Comms Huddle","Side Huddle","1-on-1 Chat",
 "Official Post","Personal Post","Anonymous Post")),
 period = factor(period, levels=c("Normal","Escalation","Crisis")),
 bar_fill = case_when(
 !monitored & period=="Crisis" ~ "#e8a0a0",
 !monitored ~ "#f4c5c5",
 monitored & period=="Crisis" ~ "#5b9bd5",
 monitored ~ "#b5d4ee",
 TRUE ~ "#cccccc"
 ),
 tip_txt = paste0(
 "<b>", ch_label, "</b><br>",
 "Period: ", period, "<br>",
 "Messages: ", n, "<br>",
 "<i>", scope_lbl, "</i>")
 )

scope_facet_labels <- monitor_status |>
 mutate(
 ch_label = factor(ch_label, levels = levels(ch_vol$ch_label)),
 facet_label = paste0(ch_label, "\n", scope_lbl)
 ) |>
 select(ch_label, facet_label)

ch_vol <- ch_vol |>
 left_join(scope_facet_labels, by = "ch_label") |>
 mutate(facet_label = factor(facet_label, levels = scope_facet_labels$facet_label))

# Scope annotation band data (background shading)
scope_bands <- tibble(
 ch_label = factor(c("Side Huddle","1-on-1 Chat","Personal Post","Anonymous Post"),
 levels=levels(ch_vol$ch_label)),
 xmin = 0.5, xmax = 3.5
) |>
 left_join(scope_facet_labels, by = "ch_label") |>
 mutate(facet_label = factor(facet_label, levels = levels(ch_vol$facet_label)))

p_scope <- ggplot(ch_vol,
 aes(x=period, y=n, fill=bar_fill, text=tip_txt)) +
 # Shaded background for out-of-scope channels
 geom_rect(data=scope_bands,
 aes(xmin=xmin, xmax=xmax, ymin=-Inf, ymax=Inf),
 fill="#fdf0f0", alpha=0.6, inherit.aes=FALSE) +
 geom_col(width=0.65) +
 scale_fill_identity() +
 facet_wrap(~facet_label, nrow=2, scales="free_y") +
 scale_x_discrete(limits=c("Normal","Escalation","Crisis")) +
 scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.2))) +
 labs(title="Message volume by channel and period - annotated by Judge-Agent monitoring scope",
 subtitle="Pink shading = channels outside Judge-Agent's monitoring scope",
 x=NULL, y="Messages") +
 theme_mc1() +
theme(strip.text=element_text(size=9.5, face="bold", lineheight=1.15,
margin=margin(t=10, r=4, b=10, l=4)),
 strip.background=element_rect(fill="#eef3fb", colour="#c0cce0"),
 panel.spacing=unit(1.35,"lines"),
 axis.text.x=element_text(angle=0, vjust=0.5))

scope_widget <- ggplotly(p_scope, tooltip="text", height=650) |>
layout(showlegend=FALSE,
margin=list(l=55, r=25, t=90, b=20))

htmltools::tagList(
 scope_widget,
htmltools::tags$div(style = "clear: both; height: 0;")
)









Figure 8: Message volume per channel per period, annotated by Judge-Agent monitoring scope. Channels marked ‘Outside scope’ were structurally invisible to the compliance observer regardless of content.




The faceted chart makes the structural gap concrete: Side Huddle and 1-on-1 Chat - the two channels with the largest volume increases in the Escalation and Crisis periods - sit entirely in the pink (out-of-scope) zone. Comms Huddle and Official Post - the two channels Judge-Agent could see - show comparatively flat or moderate volume. The compliance system was monitoring the least-used sensitive channels while the most-used ones were invisible to it.
