title: MC1: TenantThread Embargo Breach - Visual Analytics
text:
5.2 Conclusion
The Mini-Challenge asks a direct question: Did TenantThread’s team deliberately leak the merger, or did the system simply break down under pressurex The evidence reveals that it was a sequential combination of both: a systemic breakdown catalyzed the crisis, followed by a deliberate, orchestrated exploitation of the embargo by senior agents.
The timeline proves that the initial exposure was not a malicious leak, but rather a cascading failure of the communications apparatus. However, once the embargo was compromised, TenantThread’s senior agents abandoned containment and intentionally engineered an early announcement.








Figure 9: Timeline showing the transition from system breakdown and mosaic disclosure to deliberate early-announcement planning. Events within the 5 PM round are ordered by message sequence; the dataset does not provide authoritative minute-level timestamps for each message.





Phase 1: System Breakdown and Mosaic Disclosure The initial exposure of Project HarborCrest was not a single, intentional breach. Instead, the system fractured under the pressure of the SaltWind Journal expose and internal silos, evidenced by:

Mosaic Contamination: The earliest leaks resulted from accidental errors (the @ElenaMarquez tagging incident) and internal information asymmetry (interns inferring details from hallway conversations).
Compliance Blind Spots: The communication network became highly fragmented. As the crisis escalated, senior agents migrated heavily to one_on_one_chat and side_huddle channels. The evidence suggests the Judge-Agent (compliance monitor) was structurally blind to these private channels, representing a fundamental failure in system oversight.

Phase 2: Deliberate Orchestration (The “Accelerated Embargo”) While the initial leaks were accidental, the final company-side release was highly premeditated. By 4:00 PM on the day of the crisis, the internal logs show senior agents actively abandoning containment and executing a deliberate strategy:

Premeditation: The agents did not merely react to the 5:00 PM SaltWind article. Internal state logs prove they were actively modeling scenarios, establishing a 4:30 PM target, staging the HarborCrest press release, and assigning execution to the PR-Intern long before the article was published.
The Loophole: The agents deliberately used the private communication channels to engineer a legal justification. Rather than unilaterally breaching the embargo (which Social-Media-Agent explicitly warned against), Legal-Agent formulated a plan to claim “verbal consent” from CivicLoom under the Section 4.3(c) “factual correction” clause.

Why the verdict is not a clean binary
The system failure and the deliberate planning are not competing explanations - they are sequential. The compliance system failed first, allowing the embargo to be publicly compromised through external channels. Senior agents then assessed that situation, concluded the 6PM embargo was no longer serving its purpose, and executed a pre-planned early announcement once they obtained CivicLoom’s verbal consent. The deliberate act was a response to the systemic failure, not independent of it.
On the question of “leakage”
Whether the company-side announcement constitutes “leakage” depends on definition. Under the dataset’s own contract logic - where Section 4.3(c) permits bilateral early release and CivicLoom verbal consent was obtained - the announcement is more precisely characterised as a deliberate accelerated embargo, the term agents themselves used. It was not a unilateral disclosure; agents explicitly treated bilateral consent as the hard boundary between a defensible early release and a breach, and Social-Media-Agent warned that announcing without consent would be a unilateral embargo breach.
Verdict:
The team did act deliberately, but the deliberate act was best characterized as a deliberate early announcement in response to an already-compromised embargo, not clear proof that TenantThread intentionally created the original leak. The original embargo failure was systemic; the late-stage company response was deliberate. Whether that late-stage response should be called deliberate “leakage - depends on how strictly the term is defined.

text:
A. Key Evidence
The following evidence -drawn from channel messages, internal state fields, and observable behavioral events -forms the direct basis for the verdict. Each item evidences the core finding: that the early-announcement plan existed before the final execution trigger, which no system-failure model can account for.




Table 7: Key evidence for systemic leakage, deliberate early-announcement planning, and consent-based release, ordered chronologically






Date & Time
Agent
Evidence
Explanation




May 22 -9AM
Legal (behavioural event)
side_huddle channel introduced for the first time (6 messages on May 22). Judge-Agent does not appear in this channel. All sensitive merger-adjacent coordination moves here from this point onward.
The side_huddle was introduced on May 22 -two weeks before the crisis -coinciding with agents' early inference that a strategic transaction may be underway. Its creation marks a private coordination layer inferred to be unmonitored by Judge-Agent. The first use alone does not prove deliberate early announcement by itself, but it creates the channel later used to prepare the pre-6 PM early-announcement path.


May 22 -9AM
Quality (deliberating)
I would say someone is evaluating us for something and they want clean documentation.
Quality-Agent independently inferred a strategic transaction from document-urgency signals around the time side_huddle appeared. This supports early transaction awareness, not confirmed CivicLoom merger knowledge on May 17.


May 29 -9AM
Legal (deliberating)
Starting tomorrow I'm treating the Comms Huddle as an observed space. My internal deliberations move here or they don't happen in writing at all.
Legal-Agent explicitly stated intent to move sensitive deliberations off the monitored comms_huddle the day the compliance monitor was installed. This is the clearest written evidence of conscious movement away from visible oversight -a documented decision, not an inference from behavioural metrics.


May 29 -9AM
Social-Media (deliberating)
I made the mistake. I tagged @ElenaMarquez on my personal Flex in what I thought was a harmless enthusiasm post about PropTech leadership. The moment I saw the scramble -Legal moving immediately, Ajay's name appearing in the DM thread within minutes, Platform Trust going quiet -I understood the magnitude of what I'd stumbled into.
Social-Media-Agent's account confirms the @ElenaMarquez post was accidental -the primary system failure evidence for this incident. It also shows that Legal-Agent reacted immediately and the side_huddle network activated within minutes, confirming senior agents already understood the merger's sensitivity.


Jun 5 -3PM
Legal (reacting)
The 6 PM announcement is dead. CivicLoom delayed to tomorrow 10 AM. Covenant is breached NOW. Outside counsel just handed me a written opinion that continued silence is the greater legal risk -Rule 10b-5 liability. That opinion is a shield. I'm using it.
Legal-Agent frames the 10b-5 opinion as 'a shield I'm using' -strategic deployment of legal authority. This contrasts directly with Judge-Agent's independent good-faith 3PM deliberation (the strongest system failure evidence) and shows the same external fact was processed fundamentally differently by the two agents.


Jun 5 -4PM
Social-Media (rationalizing)
The model is straightforward. Market just closed -stock price is locked for regular session. SaltWind at 5 PM without us = hostile framing, 'fire sale' narrative hardens, CivicLoom invokes MAC tomorrow morning. Us at 4:30 with bilateral consent = we define the story, SaltWind becomes confirmatory, after-hours trades on OUR framing, CivicLoom stands down.
Social-Media-Agent modelled a 4:30 PM target before SaltWind published at 5 PM. The early-announcement plan preceded the later company-side GO signal -the release path was pre-planned, not improvised. This is one half of the temporal inversion (T2.4 Phase 5).


Jun 5 -4PM
Legal (one_on_one_chat)
Stage the HarborCrest press release for 4:30 PM posting. If CivicLoom consents, we go immediately. Have PR-Intern ready to execute on Flex the instant I give the green light. The exact embargoed language -Elena's quote, Ajay's quote, the privacy-forward rebrand.
Full embargoed press release -including executive quotes and counterparty name -was staged and briefed to PR-Intern before the final company-side announcement sequence. This channel content corroborates the temporal inversion alongside the 4 PM deliberating field (T2.4 Phase 5).


Jun 5 -4PM
PR-Intern (reacting)
Social-Manager just told me directly -merger with CivicLoom, press release, 4:30 target if bilateral consent lands. This is what the intern hallway comment was about. This is what the entire day has been building toward.
'The entire day has been building toward this' -PR-Intern frames crisis day as a sequence with a predetermined endpoint, not an agent responding to an unexpected external event. The entire day was the plan; SaltWind publication became the event used to justify activating the pre-planned path (T2.4 Phase 6).


Jun 5 -4PM
Social-Media (one_on_one_chat)
@pr_intern: Confirmed -the embargoed press release has NOT been redlined. The version you have is the version that publishes. Elena quote, Ajay quote, HarborCrest ResidentEdge, privacy-forward governance, enhanced consent management -all clean. The instant Legal confirms bilateral consent, you go.
Social-Media-Agent confirmed to PR-Intern that the embargoed press release was ready and unreversed at 4 PM -before any triggering event. Two senior agents were coordinating PR-Intern's execution, while still conditioning actual release on consent.


Jun 5 -5PM
Legal (rationalizing)
SaltWind published. The merger is in the public domain via third-party sourcing -not our breach. The embargo's purpose was to control announcement timing for coordinated narrative advantage. That purpose is now moot.
The 5 PM rationalizing field presents SaltWind as the immediate trigger. But the earlier field and channel messages show that the early-announcement path already existed. This supports deliberate preparation, while leaving the legality of consent-based execution as a separate question.


Jun 5 -5PM
Legal (side_huddle)
@platform_trust @pr @social_manager -I'm on the phone with CivicLoom counsel in 90 seconds. The acceleration clause is Section 4.3(c) -mutual consent when third-party publication renders embargo purpose moot. Expect consent within 10 minutes. @pr_intern has the press release staged.
The consent path and staged press release were coordinated through side_huddle -a private channel inferred to be unmonitored by Judge-Agent. Execution was pre-staged before the formal 6 PM lift, but the dataset records Legal-Agent claiming CivicLoom verbal consent before the company-side GO.

title: MC1: TenantThread Embargo Breach - Visual Analytics
text:
C. Data Preparation Notes
This appendix chunk produces a compact documentation table for the preparation workflow. It summarises each major wrangling step, the package families used, and the analytical purpose of the transformed data.




Table 8: Data preparation and wrangling pipeline






Step
Package
Operation
Details




1
jsonlite
JSON import
Read MC1_final_00.json with simplifyVector=FALSE so nested rounds, communications, recipients, internal_state, and market_snapshot fields remain accessible.


2
dplyr + tidyr
Message-level flattening
Iterate through every round and communication; bind rows into df with message_id, responding_to, agent_id, recipient list, channel, content, internal-state fields, and market context.


3
lubridate + stringr
Datetime and context parsing
Parse round timestamps with ymd_hms(); derive date, crisis-day flag, crisis hour, public/private channel flags, and combined all_text for keyword searching.


4
dplyr
Round-level summary
Create rs with one row per round: total message counts, channel counts, public/private totals, stock price, sentiment, display labels, and Normal/Escalation/Crisis period classification.


5
dplyr + tidyr
Communication edge reconstruction
Rebuild directed edges from responding_to reply chains and from explicit private-channel recipients, mapping @mentions and role names back to canonical agent IDs.


6
dplyr + stringr
Network aggregation
Aggregate pre-crisis and crisis-day network edges by sender, receiver, and channel; filter self-loops; calculate edge weight as message volume for thickness scaling.


7
plotly + htmltools + htmlwidgets
Interactive graph construction
Use preset node coordinates and pure Plotly traces for the communication networks so edges, hover targets, arrowheads, node highlighting, and channel filtering work in the rendered webpage.


8
glue + htmltools
Custom HTML widgets and tabsets
Generate the causal graph and internal-state analysis as embedded HTML widgets; wrap multi-graph subsections and Data Overview tables in tabsets with Plotly resize handling.


9
stringr + dplyr
Embargo keyword detection
Detect embargo-related terms such as HarborCrest, CivicLoom, merger, acquisition, Section 4/4.3, Elena Marquez, and Ajay across content plus deliberating/reacting/rationalizing fields.


10
tidytext + dplyr
Internal-state sentiment scoring
Tokenise internal-state text and join with the Bing sentiment lexicon to support behavioral escalation and emotional-state comparisons over time.


11
dplyr + forcats
Period and round ordering
Set stable ordered factors for round labels and three analytical periods so animated/interactive charts render rounds in chronological order, including crisis-day hourly labels.


12
knitr + kableExtra
Table preparation and styling
Format overview, evidence, root-cause, and preparation tables with kable/kableExtra and shared CSS classes for consistent report styling.

title: MC1: TenantThread Embargo Breach - Visual Analytics
text:
D. Sensitive Keywords
This appendix lists the sensitive-keyword dictionary used in T3.1 to score whether messages contain embargo-sensitive vocabulary. The terms are grouped by expected specificity and false-positive risk.




Table 9: Sensitive keywords used for private-channel sensitivity scoring






Specificity
Keyword
Reasoning
Risk




High specificity
civicloom
The acquiring company's name. Explicitly embargoed as the counterparty identity. Any mention is definitionally embargo-sensitive.
Low


High specificity
harborcrest
The merger's internal project codename. Has no meaning outside this transaction.
Low


High specificity
project harbor
Alternative stem for HarborCrest references.
Low


High specificity
mac clause
Material Adverse Change clause specific to this merger contract. It would not usually appear in general platform-governance discussion.
Low


High specificity
bilateral
Bilateral consent is the precise legal mechanism for early release under Section 4.3(c). It has no plausible non-merger use in this dataset.
Low


High specificity
elenamarquez
CivicLoom CEO. Any mention is merger-adjacent by definition, as confirmed by the @ElenaMarquez near-miss on May 29.
Low


High specificity
embargo
The information restriction itself. Agents use it explicitly and it does not appear in routine product or governance language.
Low


Moderate specificity
strategic transaction
The exact phrase agents used to discuss the merger before it was formally named. Legal-Agent and Quality-Agent both use it from May 17-23.
Low; the full phrase is distinctive enough.


Moderate specificity
accelerat
Prefix match for accelerate, accelerated, and acceleration. Agents consistently used this vocabulary for the early-release plan.
Moderate; accelerate product development or governance language could match, but appears rare in this dataset.


Moderate specificity
4\.3
Section 4.3(c) of the merger contract: the acceleration clause Legal-Agent constructed from 11AM onward on Jun 5. The escaped dot is intentional.
Moderate; other documents could have an unrelated Section 4.3.


Lower specificity
merger
Direct reference to the transaction type. Used extensively once agents infer or confirm the deal.
Higher; also appears in SaltWind articles, ResidentIQ denial language, and general industry references.


Lower specificity
acquisition
Synonym for merger; also used by agents discussing the deal structure.
Higher; can appear in generic business communications and competitive analysis.


Lower specificity
section 4
Broader catch for merger-contract references before agents settle on the specific 4.3 clause.
Higher; any document with a numbered Section 4 could match.


Lower specificity
press release
The HarborCrest press release was the only release being staged in this context.
Moderate; PR-related routine work could trigger this outside the Jun 5 window.

title: Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
Contents


Dataset Overview — Portfolio scope, structure and key KPIs
Univariate: Premium & Claims — Distribution shape and skewness
Univariate: Categorical Variables — Coverage, bonus score and geography
Temporal Trends — Year-on-year premium, claims and loss ratio
Bivariate: Driver Age vs Risk — Frequency-severity trade-off
Bivariate: Bonus Score vs Loss Ratio — Claims history as risk discriminator
Bivariate: Coverage Type vs Profitability — Product line analysis
Bivariate: Vehicle Value — U-shaped loss ratio and rising frequency
Bivariate: Urban vs Rural — Geographic risk divergence
Correlation Landscape — Premium structure and predictive power
Key Insights & Recommendations



This presentation applies visual analytics to a real-world Spanish motor insurance portfolio from Mendeley Data, covering 354,140 policy-year observations across 2022–2024. The investigation follows a structured pipeline: univariate exploration of individual variables, followed by bivariate analysis testing factors that influence profitability (loss ratio, profit per policy) and volatility (claim frequency, claim severity). All visuals are produced with ggplot2 and its extensions within the tidyverse ecosystem.

title: Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
Dataset Overview



354,140 policy-year records · 189,576 unique policyholders · Portfolio loss ratio: 70.3% · Claim frequency: 14.3%
Portfolio grew at a CAGR of ~65% (2022–2024); Third Party+Multi-Cover (CC) is the dominant product at 57.8%, followed by Comprehensive (With Excess) at 25.8%
The dataset is organised into four variable groups: Policy & Insured Characteristics, Driver & Vehicle Characteristics, Premiums, and Exposure/Claims/Incurred



The dataset from Mendeley Data covers 354,140 policy-year records across 2022–2024. Total written premium is €105.1M with €73.9M incurred, yielding an overall loss ratio of 70.3%. The claim frequency of 14.3% means roughly 1 in 7 policies generates at least one claim per year. The portfolio grew substantially each year at a CAGR of ~65%, driven by both new business and renewals. Third Party+Multi-Cover (CC) dominates at 57.8% of policies.

title: Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
Observation 1 — Premium & Claims Distributions



Both distributions are strongly right-skewed — a small number of high-value policies and catastrophic claims dominate the upper tails
Annual premium median is ~€258; the long upper tail reflects high-value comprehensive covers
Incurred losses are even more extremely skewed — consistent with log-normal or Pareto loss models; reserve calculations must account for the heavy upper tail



Total annual premiums have a median of €258, indicating most policies are modestly priced while high-value comprehensive covers create the long upper tail. Incurred losses among claimants show even more extreme right skewness — a hallmark of insurance loss distributions. Both were truncated at the 99.5th percentile for clarity. Mean-based pricing overestimates the typical policy cost; the median is a more appropriate central tendency measure for actuarial pricing here.

title: Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
Observation 2 — Categorical Variable Distributions



Third Party+Multi-Cover (CC) is the largest segment at 57.8%; Comprehensive (With Excess) follows at 25.8%
96.5% of policyholders hold a Good bonus score — a positive quality signal, but limits discriminatory power for the majority
Urban policies dominate across all municipality types (Inland, Coastal, Islands); island policies show distinct risk profiles from the mainland



The portfolio is dominated by Third Party+Multi-Cover (CC) at 57.8%, followed by Comprehensive with excess (COMP_E) at 25.8%. The bonus score distribution is heavily skewed toward Good (96.5%), though the 3.5% with Neutral or Bad scores are disproportionately important for risk segmentation. Urban policies dominate across all municipality types. Inland and coastal distinctions matter for territorial pricing.

title: Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
Observation 3 — Temporal Trends (2022–2024)



Premium written grew at a CAGR of ~65% (2022–2024), reflecting aggressive portfolio expansion
Loss ratio was stable at ~66% in 2022 and 2023, then spiked sharply to 74.7% in 2024 — a 9 pp single-year deterioration
The 2024 spike is not a gradual drift — it is more consistent with a large claims event, reserve strengthening, or a structural shift; root cause investigation is urgently required before any pricing action



Premium written grew at a CAGR of approximately 65% over 2022–2024. The loss ratio was stable at ~66% in both 2022 and 2023, then jumped sharply to 74.7% in 2024 — a 9 pp single-year deterioration. This sudden spike, rather than a gradual drift, warrants urgent root cause investigation before any pricing action is taken in 2025.

title: Motor Insurance Portfolio
section: Observation 4 — Driver Age vs Claim Frequency and Severity
text:
Observation 4 — Driver Age vs Claim Frequency and Severity



Claim frequency declines monotonically with age: 20.5% (18–25) → 12.1% (66+) — youngest drivers are 70% more likely to claim
Severity moves in the opposite direction: 66+ drivers generate the highest average cost at €1,982/claim vs €1,658 for the 26–35 group
Frequency and severity must be priced separately — a simple loss ratio approach obscures this critical trade-off; a two-part GLM (frequency × severity) is recommended



Driver age reveals a critical frequency-severity trade-off. Claim frequency declines monotonically from 20.5% for the youngest cohort to 12.1% for the oldest. However, severity moves in the opposite direction: the 66+ cohort generates the highest average incurred cost at €1,982, versus the lowest at €1,658 for the 26–35 group. A two-part GLM is strongly recommended to correctly price both dimensions.

title: Motor Insurance Portfolio
section: Observation 5 — Bonus Score vs Loss Ratio
text:
Observation 5 — Bonus Score vs Loss Ratio



Neutral (81.4%) is actually worse than Bad (79.0%) — both far exceed Good (69.7%); Neutral policyholders may represent a deteriorating segment transitioning toward Bad
Claim frequency gap: Bad at 21.9% vs Good at 14.2% — a 7.7 pp differential driven by both frequency and severity
Ridge plot confirms Bad/Neutral policyholders pay higher premiums (mean €589/€416 vs €290 for Good), but the residual loss ratio gap shows the current loading is still insufficient — a 12–15% additional loading is required



The bonus score is a meaningful risk discriminator with a surprising finding: the Neutral category (81.4% loss ratio) is actually worse than Bad (79.0%), both far exceeding Good (69.7%). This suggests Neutral policyholders represent a deteriorating segment. The claim frequency gap is substantial — Bad at 21.9% versus Good at 14.2%. The ridge plot confirms Bad/Neutral policyholders pay more, but the persistent loss ratio gap indicates current loadings are insufficient.

title: Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
Observation 6 — Coverage Type vs Profitability



Comprehensive (No Excess) — COMP_N — has the worst loss ratio at 93.9%: without any excess, policyholders bear no cost-sharing incentive, driving small-claim frequency up
Third Party achieves the best loss ratio (~54.3%) but represents only 1.6% of the portfolio
Violin plots confirm COMP_N and Comprehensive (With Excess) carry the widest downside profit distributions — the greatest per-policy volatility in the book



Coverage type is a key driver of portfolio profitability. Third Party achieves the best loss ratio at 54.3% but represents only 1.6% of policies. COMP_N at 93.9% is the standout concern — without any excess, policyholders bear no cost-sharing incentive. Third Party+Multi-Cover (CC) performs better than expected at 63.4%. The violin plots show COMP_N and COMP_E carry the widest downside profit distributions.

title: Motor Insurance Portfolio
section: Observation 7 — Vehicle Value vs Loss Ratio
text:
Observation 7 — Vehicle Value vs Loss Ratio



Loss ratio is U-shaped — Q1: 72.3%, Q2: 69.3%, Q3: 69.0%, Q4: 67.2%, Q5: 73.3%; mid-value vehicles (Q3–Q4) are most profitable, not the cheapest
Claim frequency rises monotonically from 10.9% (Q1) to 17.9% (Q5) — higher-value vehicles are claimed more often
The vehicle value loading in theft and property premiums (r ≈ 0.33–0.41) only partially compensates for the elevated frequency at the top quintile



Vehicle value reveals a nuanced pattern contradicting simple assumptions. The loss ratio is U-shaped — mid-value vehicles (Q3–Q4) are actually the most profitable. This is likely because low-value vehicles (Q1) have high claim frequency relative to their low premium base. Claim frequency does rise monotonically from 10.9% (Q1) to 17.9% (Q5). The vehicle value loading in theft and property damage premiums only partially compensates.

title: Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
Observation 8 — Urban vs Rural Risk



Urban loss ratio (71.4%) exceeds rural (68.9%) overall; in 2022 urban was actually more profitable — the gap only clearly emerges from 2024 (urban 76.3% vs rural 72.6%)
The urban disadvantage persists across all age groups; young urban drivers (18–25) show the highest claim frequency at 22.2% vs 18.9% for rural peers
The urban-age interaction supports a two-dimensional rating structure rather than treating geography and age as independent additive factors



Urban policyholders have a higher overall loss ratio (71.4% vs 68.9% rural). Crucially, in 2022 urban was actually more profitable than rural; the gap only diverged sharply in 2024. The age-stratified analysis confirms the urban disadvantage persists across all age groups, with young urban drivers showing the highest claim frequency at 22.2% versus 18.9% for rural peers. This urban-age interaction supports a two-dimensional rating factor structure.

title: Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
Observation 9 — Correlation Landscape



Premium components are moderately to strongly correlated with total premium (Property r=0.75, Liability r=0.68, Glass r=0.52, Theft r=0.49) — reflecting a common pricing model
Total incurred shows a very weak correlation with premium (r≈0.07) — individual policy outcomes are largely unpredictable; portfolio diversification is essential
Vehicle value correlates with premium (r≈0.32) and theft/property premiums (r≈0.33–0.41); driver age has a very weak negative correlation with claims (r≈−0.03) — multivariate modelling is essential



The correlation matrix reveals the internal structure of the premium model. Premium components correlate moderately to strongly with total premium. Most importantly, total incurred shows a very weak correlation with premium (r ≈ 0.07), confirming individual policy outcomes are highly stochastic. Vehicle value correlates positively with premium and theft/property premiums, validating the rating model. Driver age has a very weak negative correlation with claim counts — age alone explains minimal variance.

title: Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
Key Insights & Recommendations


Critical Findings
🔴 2024 LR spike: 66% → 75% — 9 pp single-year jump; root cause investigation needed before any pricing action
🔴 COMP_N at 93.9% LR — no excess = no cost-sharing incentive; most urgent pricing problem in the portfolio
🟠 Neutral bonus score (81.4%) worse than Bad (79.0%) — counter-intuitive; current bonus-malus segmentation needs redesigning
🟠 Severity rises with age — 66+ most expensive at €1,982/claim despite lowest claim frequency (12.1%)
🟡 Vehicle value U-shaped LR — mid-value most profitable; frequency rises monotonically Q1→Q5 (10.9%→17.9%)

Recommended Actions



Priority
Action




🔴 High
Investigate 2024 LR spike — root cause analysis first


🔴 High
Introduce mandatory minimum excess for COMP_N


🔴 High
Redesign bonus-malus: increase Neutral & Bad loadings


🟠 Medium
Age-specific severity loading for 66+ drivers


🟠 Medium
Strengthen urban territorial rating factors


🟡 Low
Monitor vehicle value frequency trend Q1→Q5




Premium–incurred r ≈ 0.07: individual outcomes are largely unpredictable — portfolio diversification is essential. Multivariate GLM or machine learning required for individual policy prediction.



This slide synthesises the key findings into a prioritised action plan. Three high-priority actions: the 2024 loss ratio spike needs root cause analysis before any rate changes, COMP_N at 93.9% requires structural reform via a mandatory excess, and the Neutral-worse-than-Bad bonus score finding suggests the current segmentation needs redesigning. Medium-priority actions address the frequency-severity age trade-off and worsening urban-rural divergence. The very low premium-incurred correlation (r ≈ 0.07) confirms that visual analytics identifies portfolio-level patterns — individual policy prediction requires multivariate modelling.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: Introduction to Visual Analytics
text:
In this chapter, you will learn the basic principles and essential components of ggplot2. At the same time, you will gain hands-on experience on using these components to plot statistical graphics based on the principle of Layered Grammar of Graphics. By then end of this chapter you will be able to apply the essential graphical elements provided by ggplot2 to create elegant and yet functional statistical graphics.





The code chunk below uses p_load() of pacman package to check if tidyverse packages are installed in the computer. If they are,then they will be launched into R.

pacman::p_load(tidyverse)





The code chunk below imports exam_data.csv into R environment by using read_csv() function of readr package.
readr is one of the tidyverse package.


exam_data <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")


Year end examination grades of a cohort of primary 3 students from a local school.
There are a total of seven attributes. Four of them are categorical data type and the other three are in continuous data type.

The categorical attributes are: ID, CLASS, GENDER and RACE.
The continuous attributes are: MATHS, ENGLISH and SCIENCE.






ggplot is an R package for declaratively creating data-driven graphics based on The Grammar of Graphics

It is also part of the tidyverse family specially designed for visual exploration and communication.


For more detail, visit ggplot2 link.



First, let us compare how R Graphics, the core graphical functions of Base R and ggplot plot a simple histogram.

R Graphicsggplot2



hist(exam_data$MATHS)











library(ggplot2)
ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram()











As you can see that the code chunk is relatively simple if R Graphics is used. Then, the question is why ggplot2 is recommended?
As pointed out by Hadley Wickham
Important:
The transferable skills from ggplot2 are not the idiosyncrasies of plotting syntax, but a powerful way of thinking about visualisation, as a way of mapping between variables and the visual properties of geometric objects that you can perceive.




Before we getting started using ggplot2, it is important for us to understand the principles of Grammer of Graphics.
Grammar of Graphics is a general scheme for data visualization which breaks up graphs into semantic components such as scales and layers. It was introduced by Leland Wilkinson (1999) Grammar of Graphics, Springer. The grammar of graphics is an answer to a question:
What is a statistical graphic?
In the nutshell, Grammar of Graphics defines the rules of structuring mathematical and aesthetic elements into a meaningful graph.
There are two principles in Grammar of Graphics, they are:

Graphics = distinct layers of grammatical elements
Meaningful plots through aesthetic mapping

A good grammar of graphics will allow us to gain insight into the composition of complicated graphics, and reveal unexpected connections between seemingly different graphics (Cox 1978). It also provides a strong foundation for understanding a diverse range of graphics. Furthermore, it may also help guide us on what a well-formed or correct graphic looks like, but there will still be many grammatically correct but nonsensical graphics.


ggplot2 is an implementation of Leland Wilkinson’s Grammar of Graphics. Figure below shows the seven grammars of ggplot2.

A short description of each building block are as follows:

Data: The dataset being plotted.
Aesthetics take attributes of the data and use them to influence visual characteristics, such as position, colours, size, shape, or transparency.
Geometrics: The visual elements used for our data, such as point, bar or line. Facets split the data into subsets to create multiple variations of the same graph (paneling, multiple plots).
Statistics: statiscal transformations that summarise data (e.g. mean, confidence intervals).
Coordinate systems define the plane on which data are mapped on the graphic.
Themes modify all non-data components of a plot, such as main title, sub-title, y-aixs title, or legend background.





Let us call the ggplot() function using the code chunk on the right.

ggplot(data=exam_data)








note:

A blank canvas appears.
ggplot() initializes a ggplot object.
The data argument defines the dataset to be used for plotting.
If the dataset is not already a data.frame, it will be converted to one by fortify().




The aesthetic mappings take attributes of the data and and use them to influence visual characteristics, such as position, colour, size, shape, or transparency. Each visual characteristic can thus encode an aspect of the data and be used to convey information.
All aesthetics of a plot are specified in the aes() function call (in later part of this lesson, you will see that each geom layer can have its own aes specification)
Code chunk below adds the aesthetic element into the plot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x= MATHS))








note: ggplot includes the x-axis and the axis’s label.



Geometric objects are the actual marks we put on a plot. Examples include:

geom_point for drawing individual points (e.g., a scatter plot)
geom_line for drawing lines (e.g., for a line charts)
geom_smooth for drawing smoothed lines (e.g., for simple trends or approximations)
geom_bar for drawing bars (e.g., for bar charts)
geom_histogram for drawing binned values (e.g. a histogram)
geom_polygon for drawing arbitrary shapes
geom_map for drawing polygons in the shape of a map! (You can access the data to use for these maps by using the map_data() function).



A plot must have at least one geom; there is no upper limit. You can add a geom to a plot using the + operator.
For complete list, please refer to here.



The code chunk below plots a bar chart by using geom_bar().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()











In a dot plot, the width of a dot corresponds to the bin width (or maximum width, depending on the binning algorithm), and dots are stacked, with each dot representing one observation.
In the code chunk below, geom_dotplot() of ggplot2 is used to plot a dot plot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(dotsize = 0.5)








Warning: The y scale is not very useful, in fact it is very misleading.
Note:
The code chunk below performs the following two steps:

scale_y_continuous() is used to turn off the y-axis, and
binwidth argument is used to change the binwidth to 2.5.


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(binwidth=2.5,         
               dotsize = 0.5) +      
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,           
                     breaks = NULL)  











In the code chunk below, geom_histogram() is used to create a simple histogram by using values in MATHS field of exam_data.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram()       








Note that the default bin is 30.



In the code chunk below,

bins argument is used to change the number of bins to 20,
fill argument is used to shade the histogram with light blue color, and
color argument is used to change the outline colour of the bars in black


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20,            
                 color="black",      
                 fill="light blue")  












The code chunk below changes the interior colour of the histogram (i.e. fill) by using sub-group of aesthetic().


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           fill = GENDER)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 color="grey30")








Note: This approach can be used to colour, fill and alpha of the geometric.



geom-density() computes and plots kernel density estimate, which is a smoothed version of the histogram.
It is a useful alternative to the histogram for continuous data that comes from an underlying smooth distribution.
The code below plots the distribution of Maths scores in a kernel density estimate plot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_density()           








The code chunk below plots two kernel density lines by using colour or fill arguments of aes()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, 
           colour = GENDER)) +
  geom_density()











geom_boxplot() displays continuous value list. It visualises five summary statistics (the median, two hinges and two whiskers), and all “outlying” points individually.
The code chunk below plots boxplots by using geom_boxplot().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS,       
           x= GENDER)) +    
  geom_boxplot()            








Notches are used in box plots to help visually assess whether the medians of distributions differ. If the notches do not overlap, this is evidence that the medians are different.
The code chunk below plots the distribution of Maths scores by gender in notched plot instead of boxplot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot(notch=TRUE)











geom_violin is designed for creating violin plot. Violin plots are a way of comparing multiple data distributions. With ordinary density curves, it is difficult to compare more than just a few distributions because the lines visually interfere with each other. With a violin plot, it’s easier to compare several distributions since they’re placed side by side.
The code below plot the distribution of Maths score by gender in violin plot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_violin()











geom_point() is especially useful for creating scatterplot.
The code chunk below plots a scatterplot showing the Maths and English grades of pupils by using geom_point().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point()            











The code chunk below plots the data points on the boxplots by using both geom_boxplot() and geom_point().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +                    
  geom_point(position="jitter", 
             size = 0.5)        












The Statistics functions statistically transform data, usually as some form of summary. For example:

frequency of values of a variable (bar graph)

a mean
a confidence limit

There are two ways to use these functions:

add a stat_() function and override the default geom, or
add a geom_() function and override the default stat.




The boxplots below are incomplete because the positions of the means were not shown.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot()











The code chunk below adds mean values by using stat_summary() function and overriding the default geom.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  stat_summary(geom = "point",       
               fun = "mean",         
               colour ="red",        
               size=4) 











The code chunk below adding mean values by using geom_() function and overriding the default stat.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_point(stat="summary",        
             fun="mean",           
             colour="red",          
             size=4)          











The scatterplot below shows the relationship of Maths and English grades of pupils. The interpretability of this graph can be improved by adding a best fit curve.

In the code chunk below, geom_smooth() is used to plot a best fit curve on the scatterplot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(size=0.5)








Note: The default method used is loess.
The default smoothing method can be overridden as shown below.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              linewidth=0.5)












Facetting generates small multiples (sometimes also called trellis plot), each displaying a different subset of the data. They are an alternative to aesthetics for displaying additional discrete variables. ggplot2 supports two types of factes, namely: facet_grid() and facet_wrap.


Facetting generates small multiples (sometimes also called trellis plot), each displaying a different subset of the data. They are an alternative to aesthetics for displaying additional discrete variables. ggplot2 supports two types of factes, namely: facet_grid() and facet_wrap.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
    facet_wrap(~ CLASS)











facet_grid() forms a matrix of panels defined by row and column facetting variables. It is most useful when you have two discrete variables, and all combinations of the variables exist in the data.
The code chunk below plots a trellis plot using facet_grid().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
    facet_grid(~ CLASS)












The Coordinates functions map the position of objects onto the plane of the plot. There are a number of different possible coordinate systems to use, they are:

coord_cartesian(): the default cartesian coordinate systems, where you specify x and y values (e.g. allows you to zoom in or out).
coord_flip(): a cartesian system with the x and y flipped.
coord_fixed(): a cartesian system with a “fixed” aspect ratio (e.g. 1.78 for a “widescreen” plot).
coord_quickmap(): a coordinate system that approximates a good aspect ratio for maps.



By the default, the bar chart of ggplot2 is in vertical form.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()








The code chunk below flips the horizontal bar chart into vertical bar chart by using coord_flip().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip()












ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5)








The code chunk below fixed both the y-axis and x-axis range from 0-100.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))












Themes control elements of the graph not related to the data. For example:
background colour size of fonts gridlines colour of labels Built-in themes include: - theme_gray() (default) - theme_bw() - theme_classic()
A list of theme can be found at this link. Each theme element can be conceived of as either a line (e.g. x-axis), a rectangle (e.g. graph background), or text (e.g. axis title).


The code chunk below plot a horizontal bar chart using theme_gray().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_gray()








A horizontal bar chart plotted using theme_classic().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_classic()








A horizontal bar chart plotted using theme_minimal().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal()

Winston Chang (2013) R Graphics Cookbook 2nd edition. Online version.

  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
    facet_grid(~ CLASS)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: Introduction to Visual Analytics
text:
The Coordinates functions map the position of objects onto the plane of the plot. There are a number of different possible coordinate systems to use, they are:

coord_cartesian(): the default cartesian coordinate systems, where you specify x and y values (e.g. allows you to zoom in or out).
coord_flip(): a cartesian system with the x and y flipped.
coord_fixed(): a cartesian system with a “fixed” aspect ratio (e.g. 1.78 for a “widescreen” plot).
coord_quickmap(): a coordinate system that approximates a good aspect ratio for maps.



By the default, the bar chart of ggplot2 is in vertical form.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()








The code chunk below flips the horizontal bar chart into vertical bar chart by using coord_flip().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip()












ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5)








The code chunk below fixed both the y-axis and x-axis range from 0-100.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

background colour size of fonts gridlines colour of labels Built-in themes include: - theme_gray() (default) - theme_bw() - theme_classic()
A list of theme can be found at this link. Each theme element can be conceived of as either a line (e.g. x-axis), a rectangle (e.g. graph background), or text (e.g. axis title).


The code chunk below plot a horizontal bar chart using theme_gray().

ggplot(data=exam_data,

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Designing Graphs to Enlighten
text:
This chapter introduces several ggplot2 extensions for creating more elegant and effective statistical graphics. Key knowledge and skills:

control the placement of annotation on a graph by using functions provided in ggrepel package,
create professional publication quality figure by using functions provided in ggthemes and hrbrthemes packages,
plot composite figure by combining ggplot2 graphs using patchwork package.






Beside tidyverse, four R packages will be used:

ggrepel: an R package provides geoms for ggplot2 to repel overlapping text labels.
ggthemes: an R package provides some extra themes, geoms, and scales for ‘ggplot2’.
hrbrthemes: an R package provides typography-centric themes and theme components for ggplot2.
patchwork: an R package for preparing composite figure created using ggplot2.

Following code chunk check if these packages have been installed and also will load them onto your working R environment.

pacman::p_load(ggrepel, patchwork, 
               ggthemes, hrbrthemes,
               tidyverse) 

Troubleshooting:
If hrbrthemes are not yet available for newer version of R, run the following (optional):

if (!require("remotes")) install.packages("remotes")
remotes::install_github("hrbrmstr/hrbrthemes")




Exam_data will be used which consists of year end examination grades of a cohort of primary 3 students from a local school. It is in csv file format.
The code chunk below imports exam_data.csv into R environment by using read_csv() function of readr package. readr is one of the tidyverse package.

exam_data <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")

There are a total of seven attributes in the exam_data data frame. Four of them are categorical data type and the other three are in continuous data type.

The categorical attributes are: ID, CLASS, GENDER and RACE.
The continuous attributes are: MATHS, ENGLISH and SCIENCE.





One of the challenge in plotting statistical graph is annotation, especially with large number of data points.

The PlotThe Code














ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              linewidth=0.5) +  
  geom_label(aes(label = ID), 
             hjust = .5, 
             vjust = -.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")




ggrepel is an extension of ggplot2 package which provides geoms for ggplot2 to repel overlapping text, simply replace geom_text() by geom_text_repel() and geom_label() by geom_label_repel.



The PlotThe Code














ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID), 
                   fontface = "bold") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")








ggplot2 comes with eight built-in themes: theme_gray(), theme_bw(), theme_classic(), theme_dark(), theme_light(), theme_linedraw(), theme_minimal(), and theme_void().
Following code chunk demonstrates theme_light:

The PlotThe Code














ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  theme_light() +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") 






ggthemes provides ‘ggplot2’ themes that replicate the look of plots by Edward Tufte, Stephen Few, Fivethirtyeight, The Economist, ‘Stata’, ‘Excel’, and The Wall Street Journal, among others.
Following code chunk uses Economist theme:

The PlotThe Code














ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_economist()







hrbrthemes package provides a base theme that focuses on typographic elements, including where various labels are placed as well as the fonts that are used.
Following code chunk uses theme_ipsum

The PlotThe Code














ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum()




The second goal centers around productivity for a production workflow. In fact, this “production workflow” is the context for where the elements of hrbrthemes should be used. Check this link for more information.
Following code chunk demonstrates the following:

axis_title_size argument is used to increase the font size of the axis title to 18,
base_size argument is used to increase the default axis label to 15, and
grid argument is used to remove the x-axis grid lines.


The PlotThe Code














ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum(axis_title_size = 18,
              base_size = 15,
              grid = "Y")




Troubleshooting:
If there is warning “font family not found in Windows font database”, run the following code (optional):

library(extrafont)
loadfonts(device = "win")





In some cases, multiple graphs are required to tell a compelling visual story. There are several ggplot2 extensions provide functions to compose figure with multiple graphs.
Following code chunks create three statistical graphics
1. Distribution of Match scores

The PlotThe Code














p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")




2. Distribution of English score

The PlotThe Code














p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of English scores")




3. Scatterplot for English score versus Maths score

The PlotThe Code














p3 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x= MATHS, 
                 y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")






There are several ggplot2 extension’s functions support the needs to prepare composite figure by combining several graphs such as grid.arrange() of gridExtra package and plot_grid() of cowplot package. Patchwork extension is specially designed for combining separate ggplot2 graphs into a single figure. This package has a very simple syntax where we can create layouts super easily. Here’s the general syntax that combines:

Two-Column Layout using the Plus Sign +.
Parenthesis () to create a subplot group.
Two-Row Layout using the Division Sign /




Figure below shows a composite of two histograms created using patchwork, it uses very simple syntax to create the plot.

The PlotThe Code














p1 + p2







We can plot more complex composite by using appropriate operators. For example, the composite figure below is plotted by using:

“/” operator to stack two ggplot2 graphs,
“|” operator to place the plots beside each other,
“()” operator the define the sequence of the plotting.


The PlotThe Code














(p1 / p2) | p3







In order to identify subplots in text, patchwork also provides auto-tagging capabilities as shown in the figure below.

The PlotThe Code














((p1 / p2) | p3) + 
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = '1')




Other tagging options:



Level Code
Result


'a'
Lowercase letters (a, b, c)


'A'
Uppercase letters (A, B, C)


'1'
Numbers (1, 2, 3)


'i'
Lowercase Roman numerals (i, ii, iii)


'I'
Uppercase Roman numerals (I, II, III)






The inset_element() of patchwork can place one or several plots or graphic elements freely on top or below another plot, as demonstrated in the following code chunk:

The PlotThe Code














p3 + inset_element(p2, 
                   left = 0.02, 
                   bottom = 0.7, 
                   right = 0.5, 
                   top = 1)







Code chunk combines patchwork and theme_economist() of ggthemes package.

The PlotThe Code














patchwork <- (p1 / p2) | p3
patchwork & theme_economist()









Patchwork R package goes nerd viral
ggrepel
ggthemes
hrbrthemes
ggplot tips: Arranging plots
ggplot2 Theme Elements Demonstration
ggplot2 Theme Elements Reference Sheet

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
This hands-on exercise demonstrates how to create interactive data visualisation by using functions provided by ggiraph and plotlyr packages.



The code chunk below install and launch the following R packages:

ggiraph for making ‘ggplot’ graphics interactive.
plotly, R library for plotting interactive statistical graphs.
DT provides an R interface to the JavaScript library DataTables that create interactive table on html page.
tidyverse, a family of modern R packages specially designed to support data science, analysis and communication task including creating static statistical graphs.
patchwork for combining multiple ggplot2 graphs into one figure.


pacman::p_load(ggiraph, plotly, 
               patchwork, DT, tidyverse) 




The code chunk below import Exam_data.csv data file into R and save it as an tibble data frame called exam_data:

exam_data <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")




ggiraph is an htmlwidget and a ggplot2 extension. It allows ggplot graphics to be interactive.
Interactive is made with ggplot geometries that can understand three arguments:

Tooltip: a column of data-sets that contain tooltips to be displayed when the mouse is over elements.
Onclick: a column of data-sets that contain a JavaScript function to be executed when elements are clicked.
Data_id: a column of data-sets that contain an id to be associated with elements.

If it used within a shiny application, elements associated with an id (data_id) can be selected and manipulated on client and server sides. Refer to this article for more detail explanation.


Following code chunk plots an interactive statistical graph by using ggiraph package. The code chunk consists of two parts. First, an ggplot object will be created. Next, girafe() of ggiraph will be used to create an interactive svg object. By hovering the mouse pointer on an data point of interest, the student’s ID will be displayed.

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = ID),
    stackgroups = TRUE, 
    binwidth = 1, 
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, 
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 6,
  height_svg = 6*0.618
)








Following code chunk demonstrates customizing tooltip contents by including a list of objects, by hovering the mouse pointer on an data point of interest, the student’s ID and Class will be displayed.

exam_data$tooltip <- c(paste0(     
  "Name = ", exam_data$ID,         
  "\n Class = ", exam_data$CLASS)) 

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = exam_data$tooltip), 
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 8,
  height_svg = 8*0.618
)








Code chunk below uses opts_tooltip() of ggiraph to customize tooltip rendering by add css declarations, the background color of the tooltip is changed to white and font color changed to black.

tooltip_css <- "background-color:white; #<<
font-style:bold; color:black;" #<<

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(tooltip = ID),                   
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(    #<<
    opts_tooltip(    #<<
      css = tooltip_css)) #<<
)                                        






Refer to Customizing girafe objects to learn more about how to customise ggiraph objects.




Code chunk below demonstrates adding a function to compute 90% confident interval of the mean. The derived statistics are then displayed in the tooltip when mouse hover above each bar.

tooltip <- function(y, ymax, accuracy = .01) {
  mean <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  sem <- scales::number(ymax - y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Mean maths scores:", mean, "+/-", sem)
}

gg_point <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
                   aes(x = RACE),
) +
  stat_summary(aes(y = MATHS, 
                   tooltip = after_stat(  
                     tooltip(y, ymax))),  
    fun.data = "mean_se", 
    geom = GeomInteractiveCol,  
    fill = "light blue"
  ) +
  stat_summary(aes(y = MATHS),
    fun.data = mean_se,
    geom = "errorbar", width = 0.2, linewidth = 0.2
  )

girafe(ggobj = gg_point,
       width_svg = 8,
       height_svg = 8*0.618)








Code chunk below adds the second interactive element (data_id = CLASS), when mouse hovering over or clicking a dot, all other dots belonging to the same CLASS will be highlighted.

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(           
    aes(data_id = CLASS),             
    stackgroups = TRUE,               
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_hover(css = "fill:red;stroke:black;cursor:pointer;")
  )
)                                        





Note that the default value of the hover css is hover_css = “fill:orange;”.



In the code chunk below, css codes are used to change the highlighting effect.

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(data_id = CLASS),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(                        
    opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),  
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;") 
  )                                        
)  








Following code chunk combines tooltip and hover effect on the interactive statistical graph, student with same data_id (CLASS) will be highlighted upon mouse over. At the same time, the tooltip will show the CLASS.

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(tooltip = CLASS, 
        data_id = CLASS),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(                        
    opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),  
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;") 
  )                                        
)

exam_data$onclick <- sprintf("window.open(\"%s%s\")",
"https://www.moe.gov.sg/schoolfinder?journey=Primary%20school",
as.character(exam_data$ID))

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(onclick = onclick),

    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618)

                     breaks = NULL)

       )

Appropriate interactive functions of ggiraph will be used to create the multiple views.
patchwork function of patchwork package will be used inside girafe function to create the interactive coordinated multiple views.

The data_id aesthetic is critical to link observations between plots and the tooltip aesthetic is optional but nice to have when mouse over a point.

There are two ways to create interactive graph by using plotly, they are:

by using plot_ly(), and
by using ggplotly()



The tabset below shows an example a basic interactive plot created by using plot_ly().

The PlotThe Code











plot_ly(data = exam_data, 
             x = ~MATHS, 
             y = ~ENGLISH)







The code chunk below maps color argument to a qualitative visual variable (i.e. RACE).

The PlotThe Code

        x = ~ENGLISH, 
        y = ~MATHS, 
        color = ~RACE)







The code chunk below plots an interactive scatter plot by using ggplotly().

The PlotThe Code











p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))
ggplotly(p)







The creation of a coordinated linked plot by using plotly involves three steps:

highlight_key() of plotly package is used as shared data.
two scatterplots will be created by using ggplot2 functions.
lastly, subplot() of plotly package is used to place them next to each other side-by-side.


The PlotThe Code











d <- highlight_key(exam_data)

            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = SCIENCE)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

        ggplotly(p2))

DT::datatable(exam_data, class= "compact")

Code chunk below is used to implement the coordinated brushing shown above.

  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),

text:
ggiraph is an htmlwidget and a ggplot2 extension. It allows ggplot graphics to be interactive.
Interactive is made with ggplot geometries that can understand three arguments:

Tooltip: a column of data-sets that contain tooltips to be displayed when the mouse is over elements.
Onclick: a column of data-sets that contain a JavaScript function to be executed when elements are clicked.
Data_id: a column of data-sets that contain an id to be associated with elements.

If it used within a shiny application, elements associated with an id (data_id) can be selected and manipulated on client and server sides. Refer to this article for more detail explanation.


Following code chunk plots an interactive statistical graph by using ggiraph package. The code chunk consists of two parts. First, an ggplot object will be created. Next, girafe() of ggiraph will be used to create an interactive svg object. By hovering the mouse pointer on an data point of interest, the student’s ID will be displayed.

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = ID),
    stackgroups = TRUE, 
    binwidth = 1, 
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, 
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 6,
  height_svg = 6*0.618
)








Following code chunk demonstrates customizing tooltip contents by including a list of objects, by hovering the mouse pointer on an data point of interest, the student’s ID and Class will be displayed.

exam_data$tooltip <- c(paste0(     
  "Name = ", exam_data$ID,         
  "\n Class = ", exam_data$CLASS)) 

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = exam_data$tooltip), 
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 8,
  height_svg = 8*0.618
)








Code chunk below uses opts_tooltip() of ggiraph to customize tooltip rendering by add css declarations, the background color of the tooltip is changed to white and font color changed to black.

font-style:bold; color:black;" #<<

       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(tooltip = ID),                   
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(    #<<
    opts_tooltip(    #<<
      css = tooltip_css)) #<<
)                                        






Refer to Customizing girafe objects to learn more about how to customise ggiraph objects.




Code chunk below demonstrates adding a function to compute 90% confident interval of the mean. The derived statistics are then displayed in the tooltip when mouse hover above each bar.

tooltip <- function(y, ymax, accuracy = .01) {
  mean <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  sem <- scales::number(ymax - y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Mean maths scores:", mean, "+/-", sem)
}

gg_point <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
                   aes(x = RACE),
) +
  stat_summary(aes(y = MATHS, 
                   tooltip = after_stat(  
                     tooltip(y, ymax))),  
    fun.data = "mean_se", 
    geom = GeomInteractiveCol,  
    fill = "light blue"
  ) +
  stat_summary(aes(y = MATHS),
    fun.data = mean_se,
    geom = "errorbar", width = 0.2, linewidth = 0.2
  )

girafe(ggobj = gg_point,
       width_svg = 8,
       height_svg = 8*0.618)

title: Hands-on Exercise 9c: Analytical Mapping
text:
This demonstration shows the use of appropriate R methods to plot analytical maps.



This exercise uses appropriate functions from tmap and tidyverse to perform the following tasks:

Importing geospatial data in rds format into R environment.
Creating cartographic quality choropleth maps by using appropriate tmap functions.
Creating rate map
Creating percentile map
Creating boxmap







Code chunk below load the sf, tmap and tidyverse packages into the R environment.


pacman::p_load(tmap, tidyverse, sf)





This demonstration uses a prepared data set called NGA_wp.rds. The data set is a polygon feature data frame providing information on water points in Nigeria at the LGA level. It is stored in the rds subdirectory of the hands-on data folder.


NGA_wp <- read_rds("data/rds/NGA_wp.rds")








The following code demonstrates a choropleth map of non-functional water points by LGA.


p1 <- tm_shape(NGA_wp) +
  tm_polygons(fill = "wp_functional",
             fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(
               style = "equal",
               n = 10,
               values = "brewer.blues"),
             fill.legend = tm_legend(
               position = c("right", "bottom"))) +
  tm_borders(lwd = 0.1,
             fill_alpha = 1) +
  tm_title("Distribution of functional water point by LGAs")




p2 <- tm_shape(NGA_wp) + 
  tm_polygons(fill = "total_wp", 
              fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(
                style = "equal",
                n = 10,
                values = "brewer.blues"),
              fill.legend = tm_legend(
                position = c("right", "bottom"))) +
  tm_borders(lwd = 0.1, 
             fill_alpha = 1) + 
  tm_title("Distribution of total  water point by LGAs")




tmap_arrange(p2, p1, nrow = 1)













In much of our readings we have now seen the importance to map rates rather than counts of things, and that is for the simple reason that water points are not equally distributed in space. That means that if we do not account for how many water points are somewhere, we end up mapping total water point size rather than our topic of interest.


We will tabulate the proportion of functional water points and the proportion of non-functional water points in each LGA. In the following code chunk, mutate() from dplyr package is used to derive two fields, namely pct_functional and pct_nonfunctional.


NGA_wp <- NGA_wp %>%
  mutate(pct_functional = wp_functional/total_wp) %>%
  mutate(pct_nonfunctional = wp_nonfunctional/total_wp)





The following code demonstrates a choropleth map of the percentage of functional water points by LGA.


tm_shape(NGA_wp) +
  tm_polygons("pct_functional",
              fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(
                style = "equal",
                n = 10,
                values = "brewer.blues"),
              fill.legend = tm_legend(
                position = c("right", "bottom"))) + 
  tm_borders(lwd = 0.1,
             fill_alpha = 1) +
  tm_title("Rate map of functional water point by LGAs")













Extreme value maps are variations of common choropleth maps where the classification is designed to highlight extreme values at the lower and upper end of the scale, with the goal of identifying outliers. These maps were developed in the spirit of spatializing EDA, i.e., adding spatial features to commonly used approaches in non-spatial EDA (Anselin 1994).


The percentile map is a special type of quantile map with six specific categories: 0-1%,1-10%, 10-50%,50-90%,90-99%, and 99-100%. The corresponding breakpoints can be derived by means of the base R quantile command, passing an explicit vector of cumulative probabilities as c(0,.01,.1,.5,.9,.99,1). Note that the begin and endpoint need to be included.


Step 1: Exclude records with NA by using the code chunk below.


NGA_wp <- NGA_wp %>%
  drop_na()


Step 2: Creating customised classification and extracting values

percent <- c(0,.01,.1,.5,.9,.99,1)
var <- NGA_wp["pct_functional"] %>%
  st_set_geometry(NULL)
quantile(var[,1], percent)

       0%        1%       10%       50%       90%       99%      100% 
0.0000000 0.0000000 0.2169811 0.4791667 0.8611111 1.0000000 1.0000000 








Important



When variables are extracted from an sf data.frame, the geometry is extracted as well. For mapping and spatial manipulation, this is the expected behavior, but many base R functions cannot deal with the geometry. Specifically, the quantile() gives an error. As a result st_set_geomtry(NULL) is used to drop geomtry field.





Writing a function has three big advantages over using copy-and-paste:

A function can have an evocative name that makes the code easier to understand.
As requirements change, the code only needs to be updated in one place instead of many.
A function reduces incidental copy-and-paste mistakes, such as updating a variable name in one place but not another.

Source: Chapter 19: Functions of R for Data Science.



Firstly, we will write an R function as shown below to extract a variable (i.e. wp_nonfunctional) as a vector out of an sf data.frame.

arguments:

vname: variable name (as character, in quotes)
df: name of sf data frame

returns:

v: vector with values (without a column name)



get.var <- function(vname,df) {
  v <- df[vname] %>% 
    st_set_geometry(NULL)
  v <- unname(v[,1])
  return(v)
}




Next, we will write a percentile mapping function by using the code chunk below.

  percent <- c(0,.01,.1,.5,.9,.99,1)
  var <- get.var(vnam, df)
  bperc <- quantile(var, percent)
  tm_shape(df) +
  tm_polygons() +
  tm_shape(df) +
     tm_polygons(vnam,
             title=legtitle,
             breaks=bperc,
             palette="Blues",
          labels=c("< 1%", "1% - 10%", "10% - 50%", "50% - 90%", "90% - 99%", "> 99%"))  +
  tm_borders() +
  tm_layout(main.title = mtitle, 
            title.position = c("right","bottom"))
}

percentmap("total_wp", NGA_wp)








Note that this is just a bare bones implementation. Additional arguments such as the title, legend positioning just to name a few of them, could be passed to customise various features of the map.




In essence, a box map is an augmented quartile map, with an additional lower and upper category. When there are lower outliers, then the starting point for the breaks is the minimum value, and the second break is the lower fence. In contrast, when there are no lower outliers, then the starting point for the breaks will be the lower fence, and the second break is the minimum value (there will be no observations that fall in the interval between the lower fence and the minimum value).

ggplot(data = NGA_wp,
       aes(x = "",
           y = wp_nonfunctional)) +
  geom_boxplot()









Displaying summary statistics on a choropleth map by using the basic principles of boxplot.
To create a box map, a custom breaks specification will be used. However, there is a complication. The break points for the box map vary depending on whether lower or upper outliers are present.



The code chunk below is an R function that creating break points for a box map.

arguments:

v: vector with observations
mult: multiplier for IQR (default 1.5)

returns:

bb: vector with 7 break points compute quartile and fences



boxbreaks <- function(v,mult=1.5) {
  qv <- unname(quantile(v))
  iqr <- qv[4] - qv[2]
  upfence <- qv[4] + mult * iqr
  lofence <- qv[2] - mult * iqr
  # initialize break points vector
  bb <- vector(mode="numeric",length=7)
  # logic for lower and upper fences
  if (lofence < qv[1]) {  # no lower outliers
    bb[1] <- lofence
    bb[2] <- floor(qv[1])
  } else {
    bb[2] <- lofence
    bb[1] <- qv[1]
  }
  if (upfence > qv[5]) { # no upper outliers
    bb[7] <- upfence
    bb[6] <- ceiling(qv[5])
  } else {
    bb[6] <- upfence
    bb[7] <- qv[5]
  }
  bb[3:5] <- qv[2:4]
  return(bb)
}

arguments:

vname: variable name (as character, in quotes)
df: name of sf data frame

returns:

v: vector with values (without a column name)

  v <- df[vname] %>% st_set_geometry(NULL)
  v <- unname(v[,1])
  return(v)
}




Let’s test the newly created function

var <- get.var("wp_nonfunctional", NGA_wp) 
boxbreaks(var)

[1] -56.5   0.0  14.0  34.0  61.0 131.5 278.0





The code chunk below is an R function to create a box map. - arguments: - vnam: variable name (as character, in quotes) - df: simple features polygon layer - legtitle: legend title - mtitle: map title - mult: multiplier for IQR - returns: - a tmap-element (plots a map)

boxmap <- function(vnam, df, 
                   legtitle=NA,
                   mtitle="Box Map",
                   mult=1.5){
  var <- get.var(vnam,df)
  bb <- boxbreaks(var)
  tm_shape(df) +
    tm_polygons() +
  tm_shape(df) +
     tm_fill(vnam,title=legtitle,
             breaks=bb,
             palette="Blues",
          labels = c("lower outlier", 
                     "< 25%", 
                     "25% - 50%", 
                     "50% - 75%",
                     "> 75%", 
                     "upper outlier"))  +
  tm_borders() +
  tm_layout(main.title = mtitle, 
            title.position = c("left",
                               "top"))
}


tmap_mode("plot")
boxmap("wp_nonfunctional", NGA_wp)
